Annotation
Today yoga has become a global phenomenon, yet despite this, an encounter with a realized yogi remains a rare stroke of good fortune. The world-renowned bestseller of Paramahansa Yogananda, "Autobiography of a Yogi," introduced millions to the transforming power of Kriya Yoga — but the story of Kriya does not end there.

This new, captivating, authorized biography of the fifth Guru of Kriya Yoga, Shailendra Sharma — "The Awakener" — introduces the reader to the extraordinary life-path of a modern yogi who continues, in the twenty-first century, the ancient tradition of Mahavatar Babaji and Lahiri Mahasaya.

The book illuminates the mysteries of Creation, Time, the Void, and the Alchemy of yoga in language accessible to uninitiated readers. By contrast with the idealized portrayals of yoga so often found in spiritual literature, this narrative reveals the true nature of samadhi and the path to immortality.

Acquainting the reader with the timeless wisdom of a contemporary yogi, "The Awakener" inspires by challenging the everyday perception of reality.

Katya Mossin
The Awakener

Information about the book

The authorized biography of the fifth Guru of Kriya Yoga, Shailendra Sharma, introduces the reader to the extraordinary life-path of a modern realized yogi who continues, in the twenty-first century, the ancient tradition of Mahavatar Babaji and Lahiri Mahasaya. "The Awakener" illuminates Shailendra Sharma's views on creation, the eternal sound, alchemy, the path to self-realization, and methods of attaining immortality. The book will help today's seekers of truth grasp such concepts as the consciousness of Time and the Void, and learn of the mechanics of samadhi and the logic of karma.

* * *

Dedicated to Devi [footnote 1] of Moksha Bhumi, Goddess of the Land of Liberation.
May Your Blessing remain with us, Mother.

[footnote 1: Devi (Sanskrit "Goddess"), the male form being Deva — a gender-specific designation of a divinity. Any goddess in Hinduism may be addressed as "Devi"; it is also the supreme universal principle. (The source footnote here contains some bleed-through about touching corpses or adjusting them during cremation and about members of a caste being well-off yet, as untouchables, rarely able to find alternative livelihoods — this appears to be a separate footnote that has been mis-merged.) [footnote artifact]]

Introduction

"Arjuna [footnote 2] said: O Keshava [footnote 3], who is he that abides perfectly in divine wisdom, fully absorbed in pure spiritual consciousness (samadhi [footnote 4])? How does he speak? How does he sit? How does he walk?"
— Bhagavad Gita [footnote 5] 2.54

The yogis say: "Truth is one, but the paths leading to It are many." Echoing this saying, the heroes, geniuses, and saints of the twentieth century have left us the milestones of their discoveries of Truth. Yet many of these discoveries remain general abstractions that require decipherment. The realities of the twenty-

[footnote 2: Arjuna — one of the principal heroes of the Mahabharata, the epic of ancient India. He was the third son of the god Indra and Queen Kunti. In the Bhagavad Gita, Lord Krishna gave him divine revelation.]
[footnote 3: Keshava — one of the names of Krishna.]
[footnote 4: Samadhi — the state of ultimate consciousness, pure awareness. Self-realization. The final stage of Raja Yoga, leading to the union of the inner "I" with cosmic consciousness.]
[footnote 5: Bhagavad Gita — part of the great Indian epic Mahabharata, one of the most important sacred texts of Hindu philosophy. It consists of eighteen chapters and is a philosophical dialogue between Lord Krishna and Prince Arjuna. The book sets forth the essence of the Upanishads and of yoga. Dated to the end of the first millennium BCE. Its Sanskrit title means "Divine Song" and refers to the specific meter of verses called shlokas.]

first century pose new challenges. The growing ecological, economic, political, and moral crises of contemporary society underscore an acute need for answers to existential questions: who are we, how does life arise, what is the ultimate purpose of dwelling in a physical body so severely limited in time? Is this birth accidental, or is it the inevitable result of previous deeds and tendencies, a planned part of an endless cycle? And if, according to certain religious convictions, continual incarnations are the result of past karma, what can be done to break this cycle? Can it, and should it, be brought to an end at all? At some point we all wonder: is there a plan of the soul? Could it be that our bodies are merely a by-product of the endless play of Nature?

Those who strive for spiritual development pose yet more difficult questions. Can individual consciousness be preserved after the death of the physical body? Does this guarantee the "salvation" of the soul as it continues its endless journey through Time? And if eternal life exists, who or what will help us in the transition from the earthly plane to the astral world? Will we be able to retain memory of the present personality after death? Is it worth preserving at all in the next life-cycle, or should we release it and play the new role assigned to us by the great producer of this creation, Lila?

Sometimes the answers are encoded in the questions: the old rarely fits organically into the new. Time itself changes, and only special adaptive methods can help "old" souls assimilate into the new world.

These fundamental questions remain unanswered even with the advent of high technologies that strive to process eternal philosophical questions — to digest and digitize them and, in the end, to compress them into manageable algorithms.

But before the triumph of technology turns these ideas into mobile applications, there is still time to ponder them. Conscious living acquires paramount importance as awareness itself grows. Some understand that the awakening of the spirit can happen here and now, while we still possess this unique, fragile, and precious human form. The body is at once a limitation and an encoded infinity; a mortal flesh-spacesuit and a reflection of past karmas. Yes, it is a factory of new bodies for old and new souls — but first of all it is a refuge, a shelter of the mind. Is it possible to learn to govern it beyond physical parameters and, most importantly, to update its software? The discovery of the power of consciousness and new evidence of its untapped potential attracts those who seek to step out of the constricting matrix and to experience the wondrous state of union with one's own spirit — invisible, yet palpable. The number of spiritual seekers is growing; they are becoming ever more sophisticated

and capable of discrimination.

Meanwhile, spiritual shopping continues to lure the willing with an ever-expanding variety of experiences to be lived. The abundance of spiritual goods on the shelves of the "Enlightenment" supermarket is off the charts.

And yet, the need for ever more unique, exclusive, and refined spiritual experiences grows. Televangelists, Zoom yoga instructors, and YouTube tantric healers have invaded every possible niche, gaining ever more popularity, tuning their sales algorithms and targeting suitable audiences.

And yet the search for true teachings, knowledge, and traditions does not cease. Persistent seekers try various techniques and methods, but the Path always begins with the search for a trustworthy Teacher, a reliable Guide, a true Guru. And this is the hardest part of the Path.

What is the measure of Truth, who can claim the right to explain it to others? Although we strive to know the nature of the Creator and His (or Her) Creation, an external impetus is needed for progress on the path to self-realization, and only a Teacher can provide such a push. But what can give us the confidence that a contemporary human being — living and breathing the same air as we do — is capable of answering the eternal questions: what is Life, what is the meaning of human existence, where have we come from, where are we going? A true spiritual mentor can be found only when at least a hint of an answer exists to the simple but fundamental question: who is a Guru?

The logical mind requires the confirmation of authenticity for everything — love, faith, devotion. In our day, metaphysical conceptions are tested with sophisticated instruments: brain waves can be measured, the state of samadhi can be documented. But is it really possible to confirm the authenticity of a spiritual teaching with a certificate, as in a college or university? Or is it better to renounce logic and open the heart and mind to a true Guru? Should this be a leap of faith, or a pragmatic choice? In any case, whether intuitive or logical, the path of spiritual awakening is not easy. It is very difficult to grasp the depths and heights of spiritual Teachers, even if one is lucky enough to meet them. The difference in the frequency of brain-wave oscillation complicates our understanding of the state of those we deem enlightened. Since we ourselves cannot experience the higher states, we leave it to imagination and limited perception to paint the portrait of a Guru.

The past century was generously blessed with the presence of spiritual Teachers. Inspiring and convincing, they awakened the minds and hearts of their contemporaries. The rapid development of Europe and North America at the beginning of the twentieth century demanded new ways of adapting to swift changes. New times required a shift in self-perception; people needed fresh ideas and methods of self-development. That is why the West so eagerly welcomed yoga.

On 31 May 1893, Swami Vivekananda [footnote 6] sailed from Bombay to America to represent India and Hinduism at the Parliament of the World's Religions. Twenty-seven years later, on 19 September 1920, Yogananda [footnote 7] set foot on American soil, arriving in the United States with the message of the universality of Kriya Yoga [footnote 8]. Both yogis were pioneers in the popularization of Eastern philosophy

[footnote 6: Swami — in Hinduism, an ascetic or religious teacher; one who has renounced life and become a sannyasi, devoting himself to a particular sadhana; also the title of one initiated into a monastic order. Swami Vivekananda (12.01.1863 – 04.07.1902) — Indian renunciant, monk, philosopher, disciple of the guru Ramakrishna. One of those who introduced the teachings of Vedanta and yoga to the Western world. In 1893 he represented India at the Parliament of the World's Religions in Chicago, where he delivered a speech on Hinduism.]
[footnote 7: Yogananda Paramahansa (05.01.1893 – 07.03.1952) — born in 1893 in Gorakhpur, Uttar Pradesh, Mukunda Lal Ghosh received the spiritual name Swami Yogananda Giri in July 1915. World-renowned Indian yogi and guru of Kriya Yoga. He lived 32 years in the USA, spreading the knowledge of yoga in the West. His famous "Autobiography of a Yogi" was published in 1946 and became a source of inspiration for many people throughout the world. Shri Shailendra Sharma, the hero of this biography, is the Fifth Guru of the same yogic lineage.]
[footnote 8: Kriya — action, including creative action. In Tantrism, Kriya is regarded as action linked to knowledge leading to perfection. Kriya Yoga — yoga of action, since kriya is traditionally connected with activity and knowledge leading to perfection and self-realization. It consists of several levels of complex mudras, mantras, and pranayamas. These methods accelerate the development of mind and consciousness and grant the practitioner a deep state of stillness.]

in the West; their contribution to spiritual revival is truly immense.

Foreseeing changes in the history of mankind, the masters of the parampara [footnote 9] (the lineage of successive teachers) prepared Paramahansa [footnote 10] Yogananda to present this yoga to the Western world.

Babaji said: "Kriya Yoga, the scientifically grounded technique of God-realization, will spread to all countries, helping to harmonize peoples through each human being's personal, transcendental perception of the Creator."

Mukunda Lal Ghosh (later known to the world as Yogananda) popularized the art of Kriya Yoga as the fastest way to attain the Divine. It differed sharply from the propaganda of religious beliefs. During his thirty years in the West, Yogananda initiated more than 100,000 Americans and Europeans into the practice of yoga. By awakening interest in the nature of spirituality and the recognition of one's own spirit, by offering methods of advanced development of self-knowledge, Yogananda brilliantly fulfilled his mission as a messenger from the East. In a certain sense, his appearance in the West may be compared to the return of the three Magi from the East — Melchior, Balthazar, and Caspar — coming this time to test an already grown and considerably matured society, predominantly Christian. When the three kings of the East came to bow before the new messiah Jesus, these magi brought gifts laden with spiritual meaning. Gold — the symbol of earthly kingship; frankincense personified the divine; myrrh, the oil for embalming, was the emblem of death. Centuries have passed since then, and humanity has needed new symbols, stimuli, and meanings.

[footnote 9: Parampara — the traditional system of transmission of spiritual teachings and knowledge from guru to disciple through initiation into practice (diksha) and successive mentorship.]
[footnote 10: Paramahansa — direct translation from Sanskrit: "supreme swan." A title used for enlightened spiritual teachers, ascetics, and sages, to honor their highest attainments in spiritual development and state of consciousness.]

With the help of Kriya Yoga, Yogananda gifted the West with methods of applying Will, Action, and Power in spiritual pursuit. Thanks to him and to the teachers of this ancient tradition, the revolutionary idea of knowing God through knowing oneself began its triumphant ascent. It is impossible to enumerate here everyone whom Yogananda's story inspired, but their achievements and victories vividly reflect the evolution of the human spirit. The story of Yogananda's life, described in "Autobiography of a Yogi," changed the lives of millions. But what exactly impressed its readers? The tenacity with which a young man sought hidden knowledge? The abundance of mystical portents and events, or the courage of an open heart and the persistence in questioning the nature of universal love? Whatever the answer, one thing is clear: we, human beings, hunger for inspiration from those who have walked the path to Truth before us.

Since 1946, after the publication of Yogananda's "Autobiography of a Yogi," no notable biographies of Masters of yoga of the same lineage have been published. And yet the teaching has been preserved, the tradition continues to live and develop, because this teaching is universal, effective, and beyond time. Contemporaries interested in self-knowledge deserve to be acquainted with the continuation of this story. The life and work of Yogananda exerted a strong influence on the destiny of the man who is the present spiritual mentor of the same lineage. Written for all those who walk the thorny path of spiritual development, and for those who are simply curious, the authorized biography of the yogi — "The Awakener" — recounts the incredible journey to the summit of spiritual attainments of a modern yogi and teacher, capable of describing the indescribable and of transmitting to his contemporaries the authentic principles and knowledge.

The Fifth Guru of the Mahavatar Babaji [footnote 11] lineage, Shri Shailendra Sharma, carries the genuine teaching of Kriya Yoga into the new century, guiding disciples on the path of yoga and of life. Not a miracle-worker or modern-day saint, but a realized yogi, he investigates the connection with the chief deity — Mother Earth. In his view, the state of infinite unity with the universe can be attained only when our own Spirit becomes our Guru, and the mind becomes that Spirit's disciple.

[footnote 11: Mahavatar Babaji — in Sanskrit "Maha" means "great," "Avatar" — "venerated," Babaji — "Father" or "Sage." The great mystical Guru of ancient Kriya Yoga; founder of the Kriya Yoga tradition. He was the Teacher of Lahiri Mahasaya, whom he chose to spread the principles of Kriya Yoga throughout the world.]

The book you hold in your hands may seem unusual, even provocative (especially to those who still consider yoga a teaching about asanas). It is a story about Spirit, about Time, about the road to self-knowledge and awakening. About the Awakener.

The subject of this biography insisted on presenting himself to the reader in his own words: "Before you is the story of my life and my personal observations of reality from a yogic point of view. It has nothing to do with castes, religions, or nationality. This is the story of the path of a son of the Earth, who values and admires Nature and applies sincere effort to know his own nature. Not striving to convince anyone, nor to condemn the convictions of others, I simply set forth what I have observed and lived through myself. If you find these stories useful and entertaining — excellent; if not — that is no great matter."

In place of a foreword

…Stepping through the gate, I instantly lost my way. The garden led me along its paths, between its trees, lured me onto the ramps of palaces, and ensnared me in the traps of distant corners. At last, utterly disoriented, I approached the doors of a large white house. On the steps at the entrance lay a luxurious garland of crimson roses; nearby stood a young European woman, weeping. Why this beautiful garland had been discarded and why the girl was crying was entirely unclear. I knew almost nothing of the local traditions or the rules for visiting holy sites; the etiquette of ashrams [footnote 12] was unknown to me. It was clear only that the standard European norms of behavior might not be quite applicable here.

…The man who entered the room was of indeterminate height and age. Perhaps because everyone sat on the floor, looking up at him. Or because he appeared suddenly, as if out of nowhere. A few things could be said for certain: he was of mighty build, moved with self-assurance, and was very handsome. And ill. The lines on his face, as if carved from pale marble, had been chiseled by the engraver of pain or sickness. He wore a white kurta, with heavy garlands

[footnote 12: Ashram — a hermitage, school, residence, or dwelling for spiritual practice, religious or yogic discipline.]

of rudraksha [footnote 13] on his broad shoulders, long black hair falling down the wide shoulders of a wrestler or bodybuilder.

Without looking at those present, who had jumped up hastily at his appearance, he sank onto a sofa that stood at the back of the room.

Later I learned that he rarely sat right against the walls — against their background his aura would become so dazzling that its white neon light frightened newcomers. Sometimes strange translucent contours appeared above his head. Inconstant, fluid, they would take the conical form of a Shivalingam or of a giant cobra spreading its hood. That day I saw nothing of the kind, perhaps because the overall impression was stranger than any glowing aura or silhouette. Despite the strength that flowed through his movements and gestures, the man on the couch looked very tired. The energy emanating from him was not quite human: heavy, merciless toward any falsehood, devoid of compromise or half-tones. I thought it was the strength of knowledge.

No one tried to break the heavy silence. Without removing his dark glasses, he looked straight ahead with no expression. Having scanned me cursorily, he was now looking "into" me. It became uncomfortable. Then the scanning gave way to a cleaning. The feeling was as if someone had lifted the lid off my skull and was stirring the contents, pouring in a disinfectant that burned away all the slag.

[footnote 13: Rudraksha — the dried seeds of the sacred evergreen Elaeocarpus tree, used by Shaivites as beads (mala) for the recitation of mantras and for worship. In Sanskrit the word means "eye of Rudra" (Rudra is one of the forms of Lord Shiva).]

An unceremonious, invisible force was scraping away the mold of resentment and bitterness that had accumulated within over the long years — the mold that had fed insecurity and disappointment. Feeling this alien intrusion into the space of my mind, I froze. It was a painful but indispensable condition for healing. Readiness to open the mind was required, and the courage to step into the unknown. I had to remain here and receive a full dose of this strange therapy.

There was not the slightest sign of grace descending, or of spiritual light. The supposed grace simply had nowhere to land, since my head was crammed with the accumulated rubbish of lived experiences — there was simply no room for anything new. Later I heard the term "Halahala" [footnote 14] — the poison of worldly existence, of samsara [footnote 15]. We were all poisoned by earthly existence, and here, in this place, the antidote was administered.

[footnote 14: Halahala — according to Hindu mythology, a deadly poison released from the Ocean of Milk (the cosmic world-ocean) during its churning. The devas (gods) and the asuras — lesser deities also called "demons," "demigods," in opposition to the gods — churned the cosmic ocean to obtain the nectar of immortality, amrita. Lord Shiva Himself drank this poison, saving all beings from its deadly fumes.]
[footnote 15: Samsara — a fundamental conviction of various Indian religions. The recurring cycle of deaths and rebirths. It is sometimes characterized by the terms "transmigration," "karmic cycle," and "repeated reincarnations."]

Or perhaps these were my fantasies, projections, and unfounded expectations? To feel unsure in unfamiliar surroundings is perfectly natural and normal — but this place was not normal, and was not amenable to deciphering.

…One by one, prostrating and bowing, people departed. I knelt and bowed, and my shawl fell, covering my face. Under the thick woolen cloth, all past events suddenly lost any significance, as though a curtain had descended on the stage. When I rose from my knees, the curtain rose again — and another life began, as if I had awakened in a new, strange world. I said: "Happy upcoming New Year…" The man on the red sofa answered absently, like an echo: "Yes, Happy New Year…"

…The audience was over; it was time to return to the hotel, but a state of inner silence grounded me on the steps of the pond. It is hard to say how deep the waters were, how deeply I had submerged into myself, or who, in truth, was submerging into what — but I had no wish to move. Slow circles spread across the water; duckweed quivered slightly under the light touch of dragonflies; the dark silhouettes of fish moved beneath the surface; some translucent entities swarmed in the dark vaults of the palaces' lower tiers. There was not a soul around, although the noise of the city beyond the fence did not cease for an instant. The mind became no-mind; it no longer wished to calculate and classify, it wished to remain forever in this strange trance.

…Why was I, again and again, wandering through India? What was I trying to find, what was I trying to understand? The kaleidoscope of life here was too colorful for any clues to be discovered in its endlessly changing mosaics.

Out of a great multitude of places, I had wanted to come here — to this Teacher — from the very day, almost a year before, when I had seen his face on the cover of a yoga magazine. I had asked no one for the precise address of the ashram. I wanted to see and feel everything myself, for the sake of the purity of the experiment and the objectivity of the first impression. And India, however jaggedly its rhythm, was guiding me to my destination. It remained to be discovered whether this was fate or a chain of random coincidences.

In the hotel room it was cold. Burrowed under a thin sleeping bag, I tried to make sense of the events of the last three weeks. They had been extremely chaotic, and the trip to Rajasthan had almost finished me off. The camel-and-jeep trek from Jaisalmer into the Thar Desert itself had not been so very exhausting, but it was accompanied by debilitating weakness from a banal food poisoning. One never knows where the ambush is waiting. An innocent-looking banana cake from a "German" bakery had turned out to be a treacherous delicacy.

On arrival from Rajasthan in Delhi, I had spent the night in some filthy flop-house in Pahar Ganj — and even this had been the best place for a quick transit through that enormous, chaotic city that resembled a noisy bazaar. The shabby hole bore the snobbish name "Kaiser Castle." If the Kaiser had seen his castle, he would have had a stroke. Stalactites of dirt, dust, and some greasy soot in the bathroom and under the bed looked so terrifying that one did not even want to speculate as to the origin of the substance.

…December was almost frosty; the morning fog crept under one's clothes; out of it appeared and again disappeared dimly visible spectral figures wrapped in shawls. At last the funnel of the city spat the taxi out onto the highway. After four unremarkable hours, the taxi finally turned off the busy main road and bounced over gravel, weaving between the potholes. The fields were still covered in the same patches of milky-white cold fog, but the landscape had changed. Now rural expanses dominated; civilization had been left behind.

…The little town was packed with crowds; dense, endless streams of people parted before the slowly-moving cars. The approach of the full moon seemed to be spurring on both people and animals. Loudspeakers howled, horns blared, deafening music wailed. Here and there, right on the road, people prostrated themselves in the dust; some ate, others traded; mothers nursed infants; many sang. Scattered detachments of dazed schoolchildren with badges pinned to their uniforms, sadhus [footnote 16] in orange robes, Gujarati

[footnote 16: Sadhu — a yogi, a Hindu ascetic, a holy man, a sage; one who has renounced worldly life and all possessions and leads a simple life devoted to sadhana (practice).]

women with heavy silver anklets, monkeys and rickshaws — all racked by an unceasing ecstasy — moved in unison, as though this grandiose production were directed by an invisible conductor. No, this place certainly did not resemble Rishikesh or Goa. The comfort of guest houses, cafés, and juice bars for European tourists had been left far behind, as though none of that had ever existed. I knew nothing of these traditions, of this town, of what lay ahead.

Apart from tea, biscuits, and bananas I had been unable to eat anything for four days now. My head was empty and rang almost pleasantly. And yet this did not stop me from walking half of a parikrama [footnote 17] — ten kilometers — in three and a half hours, in a state of complete amnesia. Even when a mad beggar began kissing my bare feet, blessing me on the path, I did not flinch. I had neither the strength nor the desire to evade the respectful touch of his dirty fingers and lips. Now the waves of collective madness gave way to the measured rhythm of motion. The cold roughness of the asphalt scorched

[footnote 17: Parikrama (circumambulation) — a traditional religious practice. The parikrama around the sacred Govardhan hill (in the Mathura district, state of Uttar Pradesh) — a circuit about twenty-one kilometers (or thirteen miles) long. The ritual is performed by thousands of pilgrims as spiritual purification. Dandavat Parikrama is performed by full-body prostrations, like an "extended staff" (danda), lying face-down on the ground. Some sadhus perform 108 prostrations in a single spot before moving on to the next, and the completion of this austerity takes several months.]

the soles of my bare feet; the fine flour of dust raised by thousands of pilgrims burst into fountains of tender powder between my toes. There were no signs of weariness — only an intoxicating, filling lightness of being. Instead of enlightenment, a pure, childlike happiness descended on me. Perhaps this was indeed the long-awaited path and a brief realization.

…I felt no particular "invitation" to stay, but I was certain that I would return to see this indescribable man, this mad place that had struck me to the depths of my soul with its harshness and its complete rejection of any unnecessary mental constructs. This land destroyed everything foreign to it; the energy of the holy hill did not feel holy at all — it turned me inside out like a glove. The place demanded total submission and openness, and I wondered whether this yoga and this Teacher demanded the same.

Waking (or rather, coming to), I took out a notebook and pencil and drew a face hidden behind dark glasses. The sketch came out well, but in the corners of the mouth something bitter lay hidden. And at the same time the expression on the face was calm and unshakable. There was the weight of Knowledge in it, and absolute peace. Lying on the hard hotel bed, I did not yet know that I would spend many years in this country, that this yoga would become a part of me, and that the man whose portrait I had just drawn would become my Guide.

…The years that followed filled me with astonishment, admiration, at times with silent protest and doubt, with truth about myself and the world. Then came gradual acceptance: of all that had happened and of all that was to come; of the outer and the inner, of personal past and collective present. The Guru not only opened to me the truth about the purpose of earthly existence and about the reasons for my coming into this body. The yoga he taught revealed all the invisible connections and consequences of personal karmic deeds that had led to the present incarnation. This was not merely shared and explained wisdom, but inner knowledge that had become a part of me.

Nothing of what I heard or saw on the royal smashan [footnote 18] during those years fit into the familiar frames or settled into recognizable patterns. The unfamiliar reality bulged out of all forms, like dough, shattering any vessels in which the elixir of common sense bubbled. All attempts to make sense of the non-eventfulness of what was happening inevitably caused even greater confusion. The stubborn logical mind kept rejecting everything that was not in its catalog. At first it kept producing the same thought, again and again: who is the producer of this Bollywood show? Who so deftly arranged all this scenery and selected the outlandish props of the "Palace and Guru" set? The Teacher's appearance was striking: snow-white robes, golden rings with precious stones, a carved staff in the shape of a black serpent, heavy garlands

[footnote 18: Smashan — a cremation ground.]

results.english:
of rudraksha on his broad chest and the assured stride… His Aztec features were expressive and full of strength and will. And even the pair of Rottweilers that accompanied him everywhere behaved as though they had been trained to pose. All of it seemed pre-rehearsed, directed to perfection. But it was hardly anyone other than Life itself who had honed those details.

He was the perfect Master of the perfect place — the last destination and the first arrival-station to one's true self. To temper the pathos of such dithyrambs, however, he often joked that he was perfect in his imperfection. Humor, sometimes even sarcasm, was necessary so that disciples might come to know the greatness of their own Spirit without losing touch with so-called normal everyday life. For one could become lost in this fairy tale.

What I heard gave strength, supported, inspired. What I was doing was changing my life. And the epicenter of those changes was here, in the old white mansion amid the greenery of the ancient smashan. To be in the presence of a Teacher transmitting the laws of the Universe meant being in the field of Absolute Reason.

…The unmoving amalgam of the sleepy pond's surface reflected the majestic carved arches and domes of the palaces. The whispered echo of empty colonnades, the sudden flashes of flight of swift flocks of green parrots, the shimmering fans of the dancing peacocks' tails, the brightness of the bougainvilleas — all of it created a backdrop for an unceasing action in a state of inaction. Here the flow of life relaxed the mind, inducing an almost narcotic immersion in the beauty of being.

The idea of writing a book arose and disappeared several times, like the beat of a butterfly's wing or the fleeting shadow of a bird; once arising on the periphery of consciousness, it would leave only to return. Without full immersion in the life of this place, describing the Teaching and this Teacher was impossible. One had to dissolve voluntarily in the heavy, enveloping energy of the master of this place — the most ancient smashan — in the measured current of life here.

The vague and unformed initial striving to give verbal form to the astonishment that overcame me every time I saw the Teacher, every time the horn sounded before puja [footnote 19], during the unceasing witnessing of the Knowledge he shared — gradually grew firmer.

Thirteen years later, having presumptuously announced to the Teacher that the time to write his biography had come, I discovered that it remained impossible to describe this man objectively. Not only because I had been his disciple, present in his presence for weeks and months. But also because the standard set of dates — birth, education, significant events — sounded dry and incidental, and had no particular meaning. The scale of his personality and the apparent absurdity of certain key phenomena of his life made the task practically impossible.

[footnote 19: Puja — a religious ceremony, worship, ritual.]

Some of the facts he himself recounted, although confirmed by perfectly sane eyewitnesses, were difficult to accept and to comprehend. Logic resisted the surge of myth and legend, relegating them to the category of superstitions and fictions.

Is it possible at all to describe the color of the sky or the nature of the wind? To some degree, yes, for there is a set of criteria; one need only operate within the terms and concepts, and in this (almost scientific) manner the puzzle may be solved. But these methods are only good for theories created by man. The science of the soul is taught in unaccredited universities, whose authority is ambiguous and whose lecturers are certified only by celestial commissions.

Such thoughts pursued me in the bookstore on Church Street in Bangalore, in the vast biography section, where I was trying to find the right format for the future book.

The shop was piled with tons of motley pulp, and many authors should have been forbidden to write at all. But the writers' police were clearly off duty, which was convenient for me. Leafing through biographies of Mao and the Pope of Rome, of Aurobindo [footnote 20] and Degas, of Bill Clinton and the Dalai Lama, I understood: most accounts of these people were saturated with the individuality of the narrators, and their emotions often took the upper hand over objectivity. Facts were confirmed exclusively by their being common knowledge.

[footnote 20: Shri Aurobindo (1872-1950) — a celebrated guru, poet, and spiritual reformer, the founder of integral yoga. This Indian philosopher, yogi, maharishi, and Indian nationalist, born Aurobindo Ghosh, worked in his early years as a journalist. Having joined the Indian independence movement from British colonial rule, he was one of its influential leaders until 1910. Becoming a spiritual reformer, he presented his ideas of spiritual evolution. During his time in prison, Shri Aurobindo experienced mystical and spiritual experiences. After his release he moved to Pondicherry, leaving politics for spiritual work. Shri Aurobindo developed integral yoga; the central theme of this system is the evolution of human life into divine life in a divine body. In 1926, with the help of his spiritual companion Mirra Alfassa (known as "the Mother"), the Ashram of Shri Aurobindo was founded.]

Biographies of contemporary spiritual leaders were either dry and deliberately objectivized, or rapturously banal. Some books were full of excessive enthusiasm and emotion; even irrefutable facts elicited predictable skepticism. The motives behind such publications were clear: the dissemination of yet another "good news," ensured advertising promotion, thinly veiled marketing. Although, in fairness, some books were captivating and untouched by this invisible mold.

A handbook called "How to Write a Biography" offered only a dull list of standard sections. Autobiographies winked cynically from the shelves and offered measured doses of objectivity. The most honest and laconic format of life-writing that I managed to fish out of the thick and unappetizing literary stew was represented by a collection of celebrity obituaries published by The New York Times. Its pages contained dry, concise, and interesting facts. To criticize such a style would have been out of place.

Disappointed, I walked out of the stuffy bookstore and waved my hand, hailing an auto-rickshaw. A light rain drummed on the windshield; orange splashes of tulip-tree blossoms torn from the branches marked the wet asphalt in bright blots. Drawing a deep breath, I understood: the book had already been written and was waiting to be secretly carried out of the astral library; it needed only a little reformatting. The editing must be done delicately, with love, without altering the pattern woven from the accounts of eyewitnesses, devotees and followers, friends, skeptics, and agnostics.

The canvas of the future picture had already been laid out on the stretcher of the absolute and indisputable existence of the yogi, philosopher, and siddha [footnote 21], whose fate was still being changed by invisible forces — the shadow-writers of the Book of All Destinies.

[footnote 21: Siddha — a realized yogi, a perfect soul, a sage, a being who has attained the highest level of the state of mind. Siddhis — supernatural or paranormal abilities obtained as a result of intense yogic practice. There are eight siddhis that grant power over the five elements. The word itself means perfection, attainment, fact or indisputable completion, mastery, faultlessness.]

Early Years

Family

The sixth child of Shri Himmat Bahadur Sharma and Shrimati Gyani Devi Sharma, born in Bhopal on 10 July 1957, was named Shailendra. He was the third son and the last of the children. His eldest brother Mahendra was twenty-three years older than him; following Mahendra came three sisters — Nirmala, Mridula, and Pratibha — and the middle brother Gyanendra was closest to him in age.

Despite his resounding name, which means "Lord of the Mountains," in the first months of life the child cried continuously and inconsolably, with breaks only for food and sleep. The unsmiling infant was indifferent to the attempts of his mother and nannies to distract him with amusing tricks that ordinarily soothe children well. The dark eyes of the little one studied the world attentively, though his gaze seemed turned more inward, and he had no wish to be distracted from this contemplation. No one heard him laugh; the baby behaved as though there were no reason whatever for laughter. Probably it was so.

His first spoken word belonged to the world of animals, not of people. He was about eight months old when his mother, holding him in her arms, pointed at a running dog: "Kutta, doggie!" — and he repeated the word clearly. Perhaps this predetermined his lifelong love of dogs. He learned the rest of the toddler vocabulary very quickly, but it did not make him talkative.

He was not yet five years old when he began to read in Hindi. Not yet able to write, he knew all the letters and would read for hours, and pore over the back pages of the newspapers with the comics. Somehow it turned out that peers did not interest him. He preferred the company of adults, who usually allowed him to be present during their conversations. The adults discussed interesting things; their conversations did not disturb his usual thoughtful state. The topics they discussed he understood poorly, but they were far more captivating than children's chatter, devoid, in his view, of any substance whatever.

His father, a graduate of the University of Calcutta, held the high post of chief justice of the state. His parents were already married when Himmat Bahadur Sharma received his law degree. The young couple actively participated in various rallies, protests, and marches against British colonial rule. In Delhi they met personally with Mahatma Gandhi, his wife Kasturba, and his grand-niece Manuben. India was undergoing a radical transformation (one of countless in her history); the political and spiritual life of that time was vivid and exciting.

Among the new acquaintances of the young couple were prominent figures of the political scene: the first Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru and his wife Kamala, the first Vice-Premier Sardar Patel, the famous political and spiritual leader Abdul Ghaffar Khan. All these people were at the start or in the heyday of their political careers and historical missions. The circle of acquaintances was not limited to politicians and activists; it also included Sarojini Naidu, the renowned Indian poet known as "the Indian Nightingale."

It was a stormy and captivating life, and both spouses enjoyed it. At a certain moment, however, the grandfather on the father's side, Colonel Gulab Singh, insisted that the young couple move from the family estate of Shivram-ki-Garhi, located near Agra, to Gwalior [footnote 22]. There Himmat Sharma entered the legal service and made a brilliant career. Anyone who arrived in the city could say to a rickshaw at the station, "To the house of Mr. Justice," and would be delivered to his destination. There was no need for the precise address; everyone in the city knew his father.

Thanks to his father's position, connections, and origin (the family belonged to a noble line), their home was often visited by chief justices, high-ranking officials, and members of various royal families. The state provided

[footnote 22: Gwalior — a major city in the central Indian state of Madhya Pradesh, located 343 km south of the capital, Delhi. Before India's independence in 1947, Gwalior remained a princely state of the British Raj under the rule of the local Scindia clan. One of the principal centers of resistance during the Indian uprising of 1857-1858 against the rule of the British East India Company.]

results.english:
the district judge with comfortable residences, and the family always lived in large mansions with a numerous staff of servants.

Spacious estates with maids and servants were a customary part of his childhood. Perhaps that is why, years later, having settled in a large estate with numerous workers, he took it as something habitual and even natural.

The habit of addressing everyone by name had formed in him in early childhood. Because of his youth and his father's standing, this did not seem disrespectful, and later such directness became part of his character. Invariably polite, he simply ignored unnecessary formalities. It was also excellent training in social skills. Having grown up, neither he nor his brothers and sisters felt even a shadow of embarrassment in the presence of high-ranking persons, of whatever status or position.

…His mother was not only beautiful but also a very strong woman — all who knew her acknowledged this fact. By saying she was strong, people meant strength in every aspect: powerful intellect, firm character, unyielding convictions, and indestructible love. Her horoscope showed an exalted Mars, and that planet of war and courage endowed the mother with a remarkable steadfastness. Especially unshakable was her will, which complemented an unusual physical strength for a woman. Gyani Devi was in many ways ahead of her time: she drove a car expertly, was a skilled horsewoman, and held an academic degree in the science of Ayurveda [footnote 23].

His paternal grandfather, the venerable Gulab Singh, retired with the rank of colonel of the Jodhpur state army, and his full title sounded as "Commander-in-Chief of the Cavalry Unit."

Both branches of the family belonged to the caste of Brahmins [footnote 24], yet their varna [footnote 25] was that of the warrior-Kshatriyas [footnote 26], and the women of the clan could rival the men in firmness of spirit. They were descendants of Ashvatthama [footnote 27], the legendary warrior-hero who was

[footnote 23: Ayurveda — the ancient medical system of the Indian subcontinent. Ayurvedic methods of healing include herbal medicines, special diets, meditation, yoga, massage, enemas, and medicinal oils. Ayurvedic preparations are based on complex compounds of plants, minerals, and metals (possibly under the influence of early Indian alchemy). The texts of Ayurveda begin with accounts of the transmission of medical knowledge from the gods to the sages, and then to human physicians.]
[footnote 24: Brahmin — a member of the priestly caste. One who devotes his life to the study of sacred texts and serves as a priest in religious rituals.]
[footnote 25: Varna — in the Indian caste system there are four varnas: Brahmins (priests), Kshatriyas (warriors), Vaishyas (merchants), Shudras (servants).]
[footnote 26: Kshatriya — a person belonging to the warrior caste (kings, rulers).]
[footnote 27: Ashvatthama (Ashvatthaman) — a legendary hero of the Mahabharata, son of the guru Drona and grandson of the sage Bharadvaja. Born with a precious gem in his forehead which gave him power over all living beings except humans, and protected him from hunger, thirst, and fatigue. In the battle on Kurukshetra, Ashvatthama fought on the side of the Kauravas against the Pandavas. A deliberately false report of his death led to the killing of the grieving Drona. Overcome by grief and rage, Ashvatthama slaughtered most of the Pandava camp by attacking them at night. As a result of a curse laid upon him by Krishna, he became immortal. One of]

not a fictional character from the Mahabharata [footnote 28] but a real person. Having married seventeen times, he left numerous descendants, and the family traced its lineage from one of those branches. These Brahmins were not priests, since they belonged to a warriors' gotra [footnote 29]. Perhaps that is why their family was not especially religious. As a rule, they celebrated only Janmashtami [footnote 30] and Diwali [footnote 31], but never invited pandits [footnote 32]. Instead, the father and other relatives themselves built the altar, decorated it with flowers and offerings — that was the only thing that could pass for religious worship. Before the traditional night of Shiva everyone fasted, but without traditional prayers.

[footnote 28: Mahabharata — one of the principal epics of Ancient India, composed approximately between the 3rd century BCE and the 3rd century CE. It is held to have been composed by the legendary sage Vyasa (Vedavyasa). The Mahabharata consists of eighteen parvas and is known as the longest poem in the world. It recounts the history and consequences of the epic battle on the sacred field of Kurukshetra between the Pandavas and the Kauravas, and the fates of princes and their descendants. The Mahabharata contains many religious and philosophical ideas, such as the view of the four "aims of life." The Bhagavad Gita, the sacred book of Hinduism, is part of the Mahabharata.]
[footnote 29: Gotra — male-line lineage. In Sanskrit, one of the meanings of the word gotra is "descendant of an unbroken paternal line." The term designates people of the paternal line belonging to the Brahmin caste in the varna system. It is believed that Brahmins descend from the first seven saints of the Vedic period. The seven principal Brahmin gotras bear the names of the saints: Vishvamitra, Jamadagni, Bharadvaja, Gautama, Atri, Vasishta, and Kashyapa.]
[footnote 30: Janmashtami — the eighth day of the dark half of the lunar month Bhadrapada. A popular annual traditional festival dedicated to the birth of the god Krishna. Celebrated in August or September. Also known as Krishna Janmashtami or Gokulashtami.]
[footnote 31: Diwali — the festival of lights, one of the principal festivals widely celebrated throughout India over five days between mid-October and mid-November. It is a symbol of the victory of light over darkness, of knowledge over ignorance.]

In general, the family atmosphere was one of quiet faith without any rituals; devotion-bhakti [footnote 33], the monotonous repetition of mantras [footnote 34], or the singing of kirtans [footnote 35] did not take root in their large

[footnote 32: Pandit — a priest; a scholar or teacher of Vedic texts, Hindu philosophy, music.]
[footnote 33: Bhakti — devotion, love, worship of the Divine. In Russian there is no term that fully conveys the concept of bhakti. Words such as "devotion" and "faith" describe only certain aspects of bhakti. This feeling of undivided and unconditional love includes deep attachment, but this attachment is devoid of any desire to possess the object of love.]
[footnote 34: Mantra or mantram — a sacred sound (syllable, word, or words) possessing magical and spiritual power. Some mantras have syntactic structure and literal meaning, others do not. The earliest were composed in Vedic Sanskrit in India. In its simplest case, the mantra is the word "OM" or "Aum," considered the first sound to have arisen on earth. The sound "Aum" creates a soothing reverberation in body and mind. In more complex forms, mantras are melodic phrases with spiritual interpretations such as the human striving for truth, light, immortality, love, knowledge, and action. There are three principal types of mantras and incantations. The first is based on the matrika — a metaphysical doctrine rooted in tantra. This is the use of letters or sounds, written as symbols, linked to deities. Such a mantra stores compressed and concentrated information and accumulates the energy of a specific deity. When necessary the information and energy can be extracted to achieve specific results. The effects of incantations and mantras based on the matrika are quite strong, and their results are direct and powerful. Another type of mantra and incantation is based on the Sanskrit-]

home. Nonetheless, the father and uncles knew the Indian scriptures perfectly, spoke and wrote Sanskrit fluently. But to the reading of mantras and the performance of pujas, they preferred discussion of the Mahabharata and the Ramayana [footnote 36], the deciphering of hidden meanings and complex narrative turns.

It is given to no one to look into the future. Therefore, if anyone had dared to predict that the youngest child of the Sharma clan would daily perform one of the most important rituals in Hinduism, such a "prophecy" would have been met with disbelief and, perhaps, even with mockery.

[footnote (continued from 34): texts. In them are laid down the powers of ancient gods and masters. There are also mantras in other languages of India. The shabar mantras of Gorakhnath and Matsyendranath arose from common folk dialects; usually it is spoken Hindi, and they contain no bija-sounds. They are more like a magical incantation — an "abracadabra" — transmitted orally. Many are written as verses. The most powerful lines, cited at the end of most mantras, are: "The world is true, the body is unripe, this is my bhakti (devotion) and the shakti (power) of my Guru, and the mantra, the secret of Ishvara (God), speaks through me." This mantra is to be repeated during eclipses or full moons a specific (but not innumerable) number of times, until it is "charged" for use.]
[footnote 35: Kirtan — a call-and-response style of singing in which several singers narrate a legend set to music; often an expression of loving devotion to a deity, and sometimes a discussion of the principles of spiritual development.]
[footnote 36: Ramayana — one of the principal epics of ancient India, narrating the "way of Rama." The epic is attributed to the sage-maharishi Valmiki.]

The Grandfather's Last Journey and First Friendships

He was only five years old when his grandfather, the retired colonel Gulab Singh, left his body at the age of ninety-seven. When it became clear that the grandfather would not last long, hordes of aunts, uncles, and distant relatives — nearly two hundred people — flooded their house.

The grandfather's room, where he had lived for the past thirty years, was on the ground floor. There he lay, weak but focused, preparing to set off on the last journey — far more captivating than all the previous adventures.

A round-the-clock vigil was kept at the grandfather's bedside. For fifteen days from early morning to late night his father read the Gita aloud, and the uncles chanted the grandfather's favorite mantras in song. On the sixteenth day the grandfather asked to be laid on the floor. By tradition, shortly before death a person must lie on the earth in order to feel its last embrace. The floor was immediately smeared with cow dung and strewn with tulsi leaves. The grandfather was laid on the improvised bed; he kept repeating: "Ram, Ram…"

Although he was very small then, this scene remained with him forever: the voice of his father reading the Gita; the movement of the grandfather's lips silently echoing the shlokas [footnote 37], and two large luminous spheres suddenly descending from the ceiling. Almost at once these bright spheres soared upward, but now there were three. Then everything vanished. At that moment there were eighteen or twenty people in the room, and all saw the same thing: two luminous spheres descended, three rose up, and all those present in the room exhaled simultaneously: "Oh, he has gone, he has gone!"

The grandfather left his body on the floor, as he no longer needed the baggage. Of course the child's mind could not give clarity to that recognition, but the feeling remained. The grandfather's departure was a solemn ceremony, like a wedding, in which Death took Life to wife. And She was beautiful, because the marvelous luminous golden spheres She sent could not be messengers of a dark and terrible abyss…

…An open display of tenderness was a rarity in their family, and from time to time he felt a kind of longing, a need to bring this love forth, to assure himself that it existed.

For some time the family lived in the small cozy town of Sagar, in the state of Madhya Pradesh. He was then in the second class and was friends with a boy named Hari Om, whose mother was a day-laborer — one of those women who, like motley specks against the monotonous canvas of village landscapes, are forever bent over a roadside ditch or rhythmically swinging sickles and hoes in the fields.

[footnote 37: Shloka — a Sanskrit verse, usually consisting of 32 syllables; a praising or religious poetic stanza.]

grass-strips, or rhythmically swinging sickles and hoes in the fields.

One day, he stopped by their home in the afternoon, when Hari's mother was due back from work. As with all village women, her day began very early. Hari Om was waiting for her, warming mustard oil in a small pot on the stove. When she entered the house, Hari took the sickle from her hands and set it against the wall, and then looked at her palms, covered with blisters and scars, fresh and old.

With indescribable tenderness, Hari Om began to rub his mother's hands with the warm mustard oil, while she gazed at her son with love. Watching them, he experienced a storm of emotions, and the strongest of them was envy.

…Returning home, he categorically demanded that his mother take a sickle and immediately go out into the field to cut grass. And when from the hard work painful, bleeding blisters appeared on her palms, he would rub her hands with warm mustard oil! This declaration provoked a burst of laughter and jokes and became a family legend. But he forever remembered the expression in the eyes of his friend's mother, and the careful movements of the son's fingers gently and reverently touching her toil-worn palms. For one reason or another, this scene stayed with him forever. Years later he was certain: his friend's life had turned out well, because people with a heart filled with such love deserve the best.

…His father held traditional views on education and did not even consider the possibility of private schools or English-medium institutions. All the children attended a state school. The first day at school was quite depressing. Fearing the change, he felt no enthusiasm whatever for the beginning of a new life, but had to submit to the inevitable. The company of other children did not attract him; he wanted to choose his friends himself. And in the end he began to do so.

Since his father was a district judge and belonged to the city's elite, most teachers did not risk reprimanding the son of a major official, which he made use of by rarely doing his homework. Once, gazing out the window during a boring lesson, he did not hear that a new teacher had spoken to him. The teacher applied the methods of intimidation common in Indian schools of that time. The inattentive pupil received a slap.

Instead of being upset or frightened, he chuckled, imagining the enormous Universe, the Earth like a speck of dust, and on this tiny speck somewhere a school — absolutely microscopic. And in that school a teacher, smaller than a microbe, was slapping him. What absurdity! He could not cope with his uncontrollable laughter, which only enraged the teacher and provoked a couple more slaps.

In state schools children of various castes studied together. His classmates included sons of servants, the children of laborers, and even the son of a butcher (the most despised profession in India). It was not that he did not know of the existence of castes — they simply were not part of his life, and he invited his classmates home. His mother received them all with equal hospitality. The recognition of social distinctions began to sprout later, and it took a considerable time to grasp the general sense of the social frame.

Their house stood directly opposite the central prison of Sagar. One constable regularly brought papers from the court to his father when the latter worked at home. As the judge's son, he addressed the constable by name. The constable's son was his classmate; they often played together, but who his friend's father was he did not quite know. The classmate's family lived in service quarters on the territory of the central prison. One day, crossing the road, he went to his friend's modest apartment. They were playing when the constable returned home. By habit he addressed the policeman by name, asking, "What are you doing here?" The friend said, "He is my dad…" The man was clearly uneasy; he did not know how to address his young guest. The situation was awkward, but the boy did not give it particular significance, and the children went back to their game.

The next day at school the friend awkwardly tried to avoid him. During the break between lessons he asked: "Why aren't you speaking to me?" The constable's son answered that his father had forbidden him to play with the judge's son, declaring that close association with the children of important and wealthy people would spoil him.

Coming home, he demanded an explanation from his father, and they talked for quite a long time. His father patiently tried to explain the difference in social positions. It turned out that even if one treats everyone equally, the other person may still feel the difference in their position, the distance. This stunning discovery overturned his world, becoming the first lesson about social hierarchy and the nuances of human behavior.

In the years that followed he had to meet countless representatives of various castes and varnas. Some came from very modest families and achieved much on their own. Others, being the offspring of noble parents, had not a drop of dignity or honor. Though preordained by the laws of karma, the rules of social hierarchy were extremely tangled. He preferred to follow the code of the heart. With the constable's son they remained friends.

The Servant's Wife

Their clan was enormous; countless aunts, uncles, and cousins lived in a hundred-year-old four-storey house. Over time several more residential buildings were added on. By the moment of his birth no fewer than one hundred and fifty aunts, uncles, and countless cousins were living in this particular estate. To count the exact number of family members either by the number of portions cooked for lunches and dinners, or by the endless cups of tea drunk from morning till evening, was impossible. The huge household required many servants. The story of one errand-boy living in the house left a noticeable trace in his memory, although at that time he himself was a child.

Busy from early morning till late night, this lad was forever scurrying about with trays, with clothes being given out for washing, with a rag or a broom — depending on the masters' orders. He was one of countless servants; no one took him seriously, but the lad himself performed his duties very thoroughly. Kind, sincere, though illiterate, he occupied the lowest rung even in the hierarchy of servants.

When the young man reached his majority, the venerable mister judge, his boss, arranged his marriage to a girl from the same village. Never having attended school, the young wife also could not read or write and was brought up in the patriarchal traditions of northern India. These customs were so ancient that they astonished the city-dwellers. They instinctively grasped their deep meaning, but the unvarying practicality of everyday life provoked their mocking attitude toward everything patriarchal.

The young wife of the servant always kept her face covered, which in itself was not surprising, as this custom was widespread in many villages. She also daily performed rituals utterly incomprehensible to city-dwellers.

Every morning she prostrated herself before her young husband: "Please forgive me for the fact that during my sleep my feet may have accidentally touched you." Having completed her own ablutions, she would wash her husband's big toe with water and drink that water. But that was not all: even being married, she never spoke her husband's name aloud. This was not a superstitious fear of bad luck. The husband's name was the name of her personal deity, her Lord and master, and she could not defile it with speech. The woman uttered it within herself, coming into the temple of her own heart. The children tried to make her say her husband's name aloud, but she never did: she called him "he," or "they" in the plural.

Perhaps modern feminists would condemn these practices, writing them off as the shortcomings of education. Today such traditions (perhaps quite justly) are declared the triumph of patriarchy, a violation of women's rights and basic freedoms. However, for the servant's wife this was the only acceptable way of life. Her boundless devotion to the father of her children and the husband given to her by God did not allow her to call him familiarly by name.

As the inhabitants of the house observed this daily spectacle, their attitude toward the woman and toward her husband began gradually to change. Although he remained merely a servant, people began to treat him with growing respect. It was impossible to behave dismissively toward one who was daily worshipped as a deity. The young woman was intelligent and industrious, knew many medicines and recipes, and in time took an honored position among the household staff. The married couple lived a worthy life. When the servant died, his master, the respected judge, continued to help the widow with the upbringing and education of the children. One of their sons became a state civil servant, which was an enormous success. The family of illiterate village newcomers had managed to raise the first generation whose fate was certain to change for the better…

The family of the former employer treated the widow with respect; she was always invited to weddings and celebrations. Later, having met thousands of people and observed various variations of human fates, he always counted it an honor to know this woman, who had changed the life of her own husband through the power of devotion alone. And it did not matter whether this love was the fulfillment of duty and tribute to old traditions, or a manifestation of the primordial creative force of the feminine principle. Together with these memories, his child's memory absorbed unconsciously a firm conviction: the unconditional support of a woman gives a man strength and inspires the respect of others. Taking into account, of course, many other factors. The power of feminine creative energy generously nourished those fortunate enough to receive it. The blessing of a woman was a magical elixir, an expression of the primordial power that had given birth to this whole world. One had only to win the heart of Shakti [footnote 38].

[footnote 38: Shakti — might, primordial energy, force; the force of nature and consciousness; the feminine aspect of creation; eternal supreme power; the transcendent aspect of Shiva — all these terms describe the primary cosmic energy, the dynamic forces pervading the Universe. The energy of Shakti is considered creative, generative, sustaining, but also destructive. Sometimes Shakti is translated as the Creatrix, "Adi Shakti" or "Adi ParaShakti."]

City. Books. Time.

Curled in the bowl of the valley like a scaly blue dragon, the city stretched its head northward and bristled its crest of the ancient fort. Gwalior was not an ordinary noisy Indian city, at least to him it did not seem so. Yes, the streets were crowded and noisy, but the air was saturated with the aroma of an unspoken secret known to all, but discussed by few. From time to time a strange feeling visited him: that the locals who knew this secret kept being born here, generation after generation returning to these sun-bleached narrow lanes, to the legends of freedom-loving bandits, of merciful rulers, and of hidden treasures. Probably they hoped to find magical gold and believed in luck. Almost everyone had heard of the treasures or had tried to find them. Hoards hidden by jinn or by kings were here, right nearby; all sorts of maps were in circulation, on which they were marked. Once he had seen a plan drawn on the edge-eaten, fold-worn skin of an old camel. On it the location of the hoard was tied to several landmarks: from the well one had to walk to an old tree, then turn left before a temple — and so on. Unfortunately, this rather detailed scheme had been drawn long before Gwalior became a city. Construction had made the area unrecognizable;

life_events.event: After seeing Hari Om massage his mother's blistered hands with warm mustard oil, child Shailendra goes home and demands his own mother go cut grass so he can do the same for her — becoming a family legend of misunderstood envy-as-love.

Father insists all children attend state school; Shailendra finds peer company unattractive, rarely does homework. A new teacher slaps the inattentive pupil; Shailendra collapses in uncontrollable laughter at the cosmic absurdity of a microbe-sized teacher slapping him on a dust-mote Earth — provoking more slaps.

House stands directly opposite the central prison of Sagar; befriends a constable's son. Calls the constable by his first name in his home, which embarrasses the man; next day his friend says his father has forbidden the friendship — first lesson in social hierarchy from his own father.

The clan's hundred-year-old four-storey house holds 150+ aunts, uncles, cousins — an errand-boy servant is married by the family's judge-master to a village girl who daily prostrates before her husband, washes his big toe and drinks the water, and refuses to ever speak his name. Her devotion gradually ennobles her husband in everyone's eyes; her son becomes a state civil servant. Witnessing this becomes a foundational lesson in Shakti.
