results.english:
the indicated landmarks had vanished, only the fort and two or three surviving temples could be discerned. Now the city encircled the majestic fortress on all sides.

According to legend, the fort, soaring high on a rocky plateau above the bluish sea of flat city rooftops, had been built by jinn in a single night. Six interconnected bastion-towers were covered with ornaments depicting elephants, leopards, and ducks. The cornflower-blue, azure, yellow, and emerald-green pigments of the murals did not fade, although they had been extracted from the petals of flowers and the leaves of plants many centuries before. The cool, echoing underground vaults were an excellent place to escape the city's noise and bustle. Like a gigantic ship, the fort sailed across the waves of modernity, alien to the new rhythms as an icebreaker is alien to ice. Condemned to stand at anchor amid the changing sea of the city, the fort silently observed it.

It was known that an entire colony of jinn lived in (or beneath) the fort. Sometimes, when the female jinn had difficult births, midwives from the city would be summoned. The midwives were paid for their work with little heaps of ash or handfuls of cold coals, which turned into diamonds or gold at the exit from the fort's gate. Whether fairy tales or not, he himself had seen the jinn's coins, and that fact convinced him of the existence of parallel worlds where utterly unknown beings dwelled.

His classmate's grandfather owned a confectionery shop in front of the main fortress gate in the old city. One day, he was closing the shop later than usual; in those days electricity was still a luxury, and the shops closed early. Suddenly a tall and very dark-skinned man in a turban entered, buying up the entire stock of sweets — several kilograms. Instead of banknotes, the buyer paid with so-called leather coins: round pieces of gold weighing one gram each, mounted in pieces of soft brown leather. The next day the owner deliberately stayed at the shop late, and the same man came again and again bought several kilos of sweets, paying with the same coins. This went on for several months, until the stranger ceased to appear. Selling the gold, the family became wealthy, but several coins were kept as a memento. The classmate showed one: a flat golden disc without any stamp was set in soft brown leather smelling of nutmeg.

…During the last three years before his father's retirement the family lived not far from Gwalior, in Shivpuri, the former summer capital of the royal family. In 1967 they were visited by a well-known astrologer — among high-ranking officials it was the custom to consult the stars. The whole family gathered with their astrological charts, and the astrologer interpreted them one by one. Over the years, many of those predictions came true; but the most memorable forecast was given to the youngest son of the respected judge. "Whatever this boy does in life, he will fail at everything." He was barely ten then, but he replied without hesitation: "Then I won't do anything at all."

The life-line, also often called the earth-line, was absent from his palms, as was the line of the mind. The heart-line was clear, but the other two were simply not there, and this puzzled everyone who saw his palms. They began to appear later, after he had begun to practice yoga. With the years, he came to the conviction that astrology was more reliable than palmistry. The changeable, unpredictable lines on the palms could appear or disappear under the influence of unknown factors. The stars, however, were unchanging.

…His real passion was reading, but textbooks and homework he hated. Having learned to read long before school, he considered books the best guides and the most faithful friends. There was simply not enough time for boring homework — so captivating were his favorite books, which he devoured by the hundred. By the age of ten he had read many biblical stories and Indian myths. The Bible and the Old Testament were like gripping thrillers. Reading about the adventures of Jesus, he felt for Him, but most of all he was impressed by the physical strength of Samson. Jack London did not protest at all against sharing a bookshelf with Shakespeare and Voltaire; the great writers carried one into journeys, opening enthralling worlds. At about seventeen he came across Richard Bach's "Jonathan Livingston Seagull" [footnote 39] and loved this book forever, remembering it almost by heart — though he truly understood its meaning only years later. And then there were countless comics. They inflamed the imagination, filled it with ideas, gave rise to sudden turns of thought, and developed the capacity to evaluate the most fantastic plots. At a certain moment it became clear to him that everything around him — houses, bridges, cars, manmade objects, scientific inventions — were products of someone's imagination. Imagination, generated by the power of the mind, was the most powerful energy in the world. When years later he was asked about the phenomenon of so-called "willpower," he answered that it was nothing other than controlled imagination, whose strong current generates enormous energy. By directing this energy, one can move worlds. This world, dreamt up by the Creator, was only one of His dreams of the Universe. The worlds dreamt by the Creator lived and developed independently of the Dreamer's will. And he, too, was part of the great Dream, but he wanted to wake up…

[footnote 39: "Jonathan Livingston Seagull" — a novella by the American writer Richard Bach. A story of a seagull who tries to learn about life, flight, and self-improvement. First published in 1970, it became an instant hit; by the end of 1972 more than a million copies had been printed, which placed the book at the top of the bestseller list.]

…The best definition of life he had encountered belonged to the well-known American author Neil Gaiman. The heroine of the comic was Death, and one of her "wards" asked: "You know more about Life than anyone else. Tell me, what is it?" "Life is an incurable sexually-transmitted disease with an inevitable lethal outcome…" The laconic definition rang absolutely true, though dry facts were not always the voices of Truth. Life was something much, much greater.

Despite his enthusiasm for reading, the reputation of a mediocre pupil stuck to him, and he barely passed his final examinations at the college. The parents lamented: "He won't lift a finger to get a proper education! What awaits him in the future?" His father predicted a dizzying career as a beggar. Listening politely to yet another portion of parental admonition, he would take a new book from the shelf.

In the upper classes he and a cousin began to skip lessons, riding off on bicycles to the surrounding hills, where they would sit for hours, gazing at the domes of the Birla Mandir rising above the city, the upper lake, and the slums of Shimla. At sunset Gwalior resembled an enormous luminous hive; the honeycombs of houses exuded a golden, fragrant honey of life, and everything around was glued together by this thick, invisible substance. On the way back they raced their bicycles through the tangled lanes of the old part of the city, past the bazaar "Maharaj Bada," whose flickering multicolored lights turned the shops into chests of treasure, as if the bygone era had not quite gone. The mosaic of hundreds of windows — shards of a gigantic broken mirror — glowed in the descending night, and the rising moon looked into them. All the objects reflected the world from different angles, and it seemed different, yet always stunningly whole and harmonious.

The distant echo of the past and the chaotic horns of the present plaited themselves into the names of the future. The world was huge, but not frightening. His thoughts were scattered, and to put all his emotions in order was impossible, though there was no need to hurry into grasping the order of things. An invisible mighty force pervaded everything around him, and although he could not pinpoint its source, its pulse was recognizable in everything. The annual harvests and the turning of the seasons, the mothers with infants in their arms, the rowdy adolescents and dried-up old men on the thresholds of houses, the colors and the smells, the sounds and the outlines — this whole ever-changing spectrum of the worldly kaleidoscope was familiar and comfortable. But the roots of that very familiarity grew from beyond, from outside the world he knew. The vast retort of Life swayed in the blinding Void to the beat of Time, and he instinctively guessed at the magical nature of the inaudible melody, but to correlate and to measure his own essence against the amplitude of its sounds — that he could not yet do.

He did not yet know that Time would become the theme of his first book.

Epic Tales from the Life of Gulab Singh

…The Hindu calendar of Vikram Samvat [footnote 40], based on solar years and lunar months, runs ahead of the Western Gregorian by about fifty-seven years. Like the whole country, their family lived by the Indian calendar; when the grandfather told of military adventures in 1976, all understood that the events took place at the turn of the century.

In the time of British rule their great-grandfather Tahat Singh was the jagirdar of the principality of Kaithal. A jagir was then a kind of feudal land-holding, or life-estate. The appointed jagirdar was granted the authority to manage the estates and collect taxes; part of the collected dues constituted his income, while the rest went to the treasury. As a mighty landholder, treasurer, and defender of the borders, the jagirdar was responsible for the welfare of his subjects. Tahat Singh took his duties very seriously.

[footnote 40: Vikram Samvat — the traditional historical Hindu calendar, which uses lunar months and solar sidereal years. Introduced by King Vikramaditya in 57 BCE.]

…One day, returning from a routine inspection of the fields, he came upon a group of British soldiers trying to outrage a village girl. The jagirdar intervened; the intervention turned into a battle, and soon seven English soldiers lay dead — Tahat Singh wielded a sword masterfully. The weeping girl ran off to the village; the dismounted jagirdar calmed his horse. He regretted nothing. The bodies of the soldiers were hung before the main gate of Fort Kaithal, and Lord Dufferin, then Viceroy of India, did not like this at all. Troops were sent to Kaithal, and Tahat Singh's small army was defeated. He and his family were ordered to leave the city by sunset.

Ordinarily, jagirs together with all their subjects passed by inheritance; however, Tahat Singh, the de facto ruler of Kaithal, forever lost his right to the principality. The family rode out of the town with hastily gathered belongings.

By then Tahat Singh was already a grandfather. His son Bhagavan Singh had three sons, and Gulab Singh was the youngest. He remembered well the day his grandfather rode out of the fort, leading a small caravan behind him. Sitting in the saddle with his back as straight as ever, Tahat Singh did not even look back at the abandoned fort.

They were not reduced to beggary, the family had means of subsistence. In 1888, after the loss of the principality, the great-grandfather bought five villages in the Agra district, again becoming a landowner. Farming brought enough to meet basic needs, and yet the men of the family could not allow themselves to be idle. Everyone had to confront the new realities of life, and at some moment

results.english:
Gulab Singh entered service. He had received a good education, and so was immediately hired as a telegraph operator in a remote district of Assam. But soon Kala-azar — the black fever — broke out there. First one of his servants fell ill, and then people began dying by the tens. Gulab Singh sent desperate SOS signals to his superiors, but help never came. Although many died in the area, he survived. After conducting the funeral rites for the dead, Gulab Singh telegraphed his resignation to the head office. And he set off on foot toward Burma, away from the epidemic. He had no definite plans.

Reaching one of the border towns, exhausted by the long road, he sat down in the shade of a tree to give his blistered feet some rest at last.

…A wealthy merchant was watching the fruitless efforts of his workers to subdue a newly purchased unbroken stallion. Seeing that the would-be riders neither could, nor really tried, to master the enraged animal, he fell into a violent irritation.

Gulab Singh addressed him: "May I try, sir?"

The merchant looked doubtfully at the lean young man with hollow cheeks: "You look ready to die of hunger — how can you tame stallions…"

"Allow me to try, and see for yourself." Gulab Singh belonged to a noble line and, besides his excellent education, was a master of horsemanship. Speaking softly to the stallion, he began to lead it in a circle, calming it. Then, choosing the right moment, with a single leap he vaulted into the saddle, and the stallion launched into a frenzied gallop. After about ten kilometers, both were exhausted. The stallion shifted to a slow trot and soon submitted entirely to the rider. Approaching the square, Gulab Singh lost consciousness from fatigue, but did not fall from the lathered horse. The merchant ordered the young man carried to his house and allowed to rest. In the evening they got into conversation, and the merchant was impressed at how fluently his guest spoke Sanskrit, Persian, and English. Gulab Singh was immediately taken on as manager for timber export, with the additional duty of reading the Bhagavata Purana to the boss's elderly mother.

Several months passed. One evening, as usual, he was reading aloud. The elderly woman, seated in her chair, breathed evenly, and it was unclear whether she was listening or sleeping. This did not trouble him; he was absorbed in the sounds of the Sanskrit shlokas, which had on him simultaneously a trance-inducing and a sobering effect. Reaching the section where Shri Krishna instructs Uddhava [footnote 41], Gulab Singh experienced an upsurge of spiritual illumination and sank into a state of vairagya [footnote 42]. He was overcome by a strange indifference and detachment from all things worldly. The "ascetic disinterest" in everything that could provoke attachment or even dependence in most people filled him to the brim. Like the sting of a wasp, this sensation pierced his heart with the needle of longing: for another life, for other knowledge, for renunciation of the pain of being and of worldly pleasures. Soon he left Burma, setting off for the Kumbh Mela [footnote 43] at Allahabad [footnote 44]. In search of a Guru.

[footnote 41: Uddhava — devotee of Lord Krishna, the figure of the Uddhava Gita (the Bhagavata Purana), to whom Krishna explains bhakti and yoga. Uddhava is said to have been Krishna's childhood friend; in some texts he is mentioned as his cousin.]
[footnote 42: Vairagya — the practice of non-attachment to the external world, renunciation of pleasure and pain in order to attain liberation and to halt the wheel of births and deaths.]
[footnote 43: Kumbh Mela — a major Hindu pilgrimage festival. Celebrated once every twelve years in honor of every completed cycle of the planet Jupiter, or Brihaspati. Traditionally this takes place at four places of pilgrimage on the banks of rivers: at Allahabad (at the confluence of the Ganges, Yamuna, and Sarasvati), at Haridwar (on the banks of the Ganges), at Nashik (on the river Godavari), and finally at Ujjain (on the river Shipra). The festival is marked by ritual bathing, numerous fairs, educational and religious talks with saints, mass gatherings of monks and ascetics, and various entertainment programs. It is believed that bathing in the rivers washes away past mistakes and sins. Traditionally the founding of the Kumbh Mela is attributed to the 8th-century philosopher and saint Adi Shankara. However, no historical evidence of mass pilgrimages under the name "Kumbh Mela" prior to the 19th century has been discovered. In Sanskrit "Kumbha" literally means "pitcher, pot." The word "mela" means "to unite, to join, to meet, to move together," especially in the context of public festivals. Thus, Kumbh Mela — a "gathering, meeting, union" — takes place beside sacred waters.]
[footnote 44: Allahabad (or Ilahabad, now known as Prayagraj) — a metropolis in Uttar Pradesh, located near the Triveni Sangam, the "confluence of the three rivers" Ganges, Yamuna, and Sarasvati. It was known as Prayag, which means "place of sacrifice." Legends and scriptures say that the Creator Brahma performed the very first sacrifice (yajna) here. One of the world's oldest cities, mentioned in the ancient Vedas, Prayag was revered as a sacred city. The Mughal emperor Akbar visited the region in 1575 and was so impressed by the city's strategic position that he ordered a fort built there. The construction was completed by 1584 and named "the Abode of Allah," later, under Shah Jahan, officially renamed Allahabad. The city has great religious significance; the historical Prayag Kumbh Mela is held here every twelve years. In the Mahabharata, bathing at Prayag is mentioned as a means of repentance for past sins. For many years Prayag was the place where the ashes of national leaders, including Mahatma Gandhi, were scattered.]

At that time there lived in Allahabad a renowned sage, the spiritual mentor of the maharaja [footnote 45] of Patiala — Rajendra Singh. Gulab Singh attempted to attend his darshan, but it was nearly impossible to obtain an audience with the Guru — he was refused time after time. Gulab Singh's fate was changed by a telegram sent by the maharaja of Patiala to his mentor. The message was in English, and the sadhu bringing the telegram could not read it. Realizing that this was his chance, Gulab Singh volunteered to translate the message.

"Just do it!"

"I will read it to the Guru only in person."

He was brought before the sage, and Gulab Singh translated the telegram without faltering from English into impeccable Sanskrit — and asked for initiation. He received it. A few more months passed; he moved with the Guru to the ashram in Patiala and remained there for a long time. The Teacher was a silent and modest man, who wore only a loincloth and spent his days observing ants, children, birds, and playing puppies. Yet the Guru's wisdom was boundless, and everything he taught Gulab Singh remained with the disciple to his last breath.

[footnote 45: Maharaja — the title of "great king."]

Gulab Singh, possessing a lively mind and strong zeal, was after some time appointed mahant (head) of the ashram in Patiala. There were whispers that he might even become the Guru's successor. He now wore garments of fine cloth, food was served to him on silver dishes — but inner dissatisfaction and unease grew. He asked himself: "And what next? There is no trace of vairagya here, this is all imitation, a masquerade. What am I doing here?" He asked permission to leave for a time, and the Guru said: "Go. Whatever is to be will not be missed."

…On the train, he was recognized by a ticket-inspector, and Gulab Singh was detained at the station on a trivial pretext; the inspector contacted his family, who had been searching for him all this time. The arriving brothers almost forcibly took the prodigal son to his parents' house, where he was warmly received by his happy father and mother. But a wandering monk who had renounced the world held little interest for anyone, and within a month he was forcibly married.

Time passed, and Gulab Singh returned to the ashram to tell the Guru of the unexpected change in his life. The sage said: "Now this is your destiny. Fulfill your worldly duties, and then, when you wish to return to the ashram, come." The Teacher saw Gulab Singh's destiny and knew that karma is sometimes too strong to fight. To make a sadhu out of a wandering Brahmin with a Kshatriya soul was as hopeless an undertaking as trying to drink water out of a sieve.

For Gulab Singh began what is called normal life, and he tried honestly to adapt to it. The five villages bought by the great-grandfather brought in some income. But the cool wind of renunciation kept ruffling Gulab Singh's thoughts; vairagya still nested in his heart, and at some point he gave the land over to the farmers.

…An opportunity arose to enter a prestigious post, and Gulab Singh decided to enlist the support of the prince of Jodhpur, his former classmate and good friend. The prince presented him to his father, the maharaja of Jodhpur. His Highness said that the position in question was beneath the status of a jagirdar's son, but he could appoint him colonel of the cavalry. Gulab Singh was just over twenty, and the adrenaline of an adventurer pulsed wildly in his blood. He took part in the Afghan War of 1892, and a letter written in Queen Victoria's own hand stated that "…Colonel Gulab Singh is a hero of the Afghan War, possessing all outstanding military qualities…"

However, Colonel G. Singh was not consistent in his career advancement. After the war he left the service and returned to the village. And soon he again began to feel inner restlessness. At this same time he became acquainted with an underground movement whose participants struggled against British rule and were planning to rob a bank. Or, more precisely, a peaceful expropriation.

In Calcutta, Gulab Singh managed to imitate the signature of an important British official so deftly that on the forged promissory notes the conspirators received 200,000 rupees — fabulous money for that time. 170,000 rupees were handed over to the revolutionary leader Rasbehari Bose, who later fled to Japan; Gulab Singh returned to the village with the tidy sum of 30,000 rupees.

In Shivram-ki-Garhi (their ancestral estate on the bank of the Yamuna), it was impossible to conceal such wealth for long, and envious relatives reported him to the police. The promissory notes and most of the money were confiscated, and Gulab Singh was arrested and sent to Calcutta for a public trial.

Indian nationalists were accused of "waging hostilities against the government," and the grand trial of the revolutionaries lasted a whole year: from May 1908 to May 1909. In Gulab Singh's case the prosecution could not prove that the signatures belonged to him precisely — they had been forged so skillfully that no one could distinguish them from the original. The lack of convincing evidence led to the complete dismissal of the charges. The policeman who had arrested Gulab Singh was also present in the courtroom. Leaving the dock and passing by this officer, Gulab Singh said quietly to him: "I did it, and now I'm going free." The officer answered: "Lucky one…" And indeed, luck accompanied him in practically everything.

Awaiting trial in Calcutta, Gulab Singh became acquainted in prison with Shri Aurobindo. There the future renowned yogi and founder of integral yoga experienced his first mystical and spiritual experiences. In Aurobindo's case, too, no compelling evidence was found, and the future maharishi was released. Soon Aurobindo Ghosh left politics for the spiritual path and moved to Pondicherry, while Gulab Singh returned to his village — but maintained a correspondence with Shri Aurobindo for many long years.

The entire life of Gulab Singh was a chain of challenges to the most unusual circumstances. One day the maharaja of Jodhpur was preparing for a meeting with the Viceroy of India, where he was to appear in full ceremonial dress. They brought him his parade sword, but it was firmly stuck in the scabbard. The tall Rajasthani guards of the watch could not solve the problem, and Gulab Singh, who happened to be in the palace that day and observed the fruitless attempts, volunteered: "Your Highness, may I try?" The officers smiled discreetly: compared with the broad-shouldered, hefty guards, Colonel Singh was of rather slight build. Hanging the sword on a ceiling-hook with the scabbard upward, Gulab Singh put his full weight on the hilt, and the blade slipped out with the soft sound of awakened

life_events.event: Gulab Singh, having lost his jagir-inheritance, takes work as telegraph operator in a remote Assam district; survives a Kala-azar (black fever) epidemic that kills tens around him; conducts funeral rites for the dead and resigns by telegraph, walking on foot toward Burma.

At a border town, Gulab Singh tames a wealthy merchant's unbroken stallion in a ten-kilometer gallop, then collapses from exhaustion; the impressed merchant hires him as timber-export manager and to read the Bhagavata Purana aloud to his elderly mother.
Reading the Uddhava-Gita section of the Bhagavata Purana, Gulab Singh sinks into vairagya — leaves Burma for Kumbh Mela at Allahabad in search of a Guru.

After being refused audience repeatedly, Gulab Singh wins entry to Rajendra Singh (spiritual mentor of the maharaja of Patiala) by translating the maharaja's English telegram into impeccable Sanskrit in his presence — receives initiation on the spot.

Becomes mahant of the Patiala ashram, but, disturbed by the absence of true vairagya in the silver-dishes lifestyle, takes leave; on the train a ticket-inspector recognizes him, alerts his family; brothers return him home where he is forcibly married within a month.
Guru tells him: 'Now this is your destiny — fulfill worldly duties; when you wish to return, come.' Gulab Singh, restless, eventually gives his land to the farmers.

At about age 20, Gulab Singh secures a colonelcy of the Jodhpur cavalry via his prince-classmate; participates in the Afghan War of 1892; Queen Victoria writes a letter calling him 'a hero of the Afghan War.'

Joins an anti-British underground; forges the signature of a major British official to extract 200,000 rupees in Calcutta — gives 170,000 rupees to revolutionary Rasbehari Bose (who later flees to Japan) and brings 30,000 rupees home to Shivram-ki-Garhi (the family estate on the Yamuna).

Envious relatives denounce him; arrested and tried in Calcutta as part of the grand nationalist trial of May 1908 — May 1909; acquitted because the forgery was too perfect to attribute to him; passing the arresting officer he says quietly 'I did it, and now I'm going free.'

In Calcutta prison Gulab Singh meets Shri Aurobindo, who experiences there his first mystical experiences; both are acquitted; Aurobindo leaves for Pondicherry; the two maintain correspondence for many years.

At the Jodhpur palace, Gulab Singh frees the maharaja's parade-sword stuck in its scabbard before a vice-regal meeting — by hanging it on a ceiling-hook and using his own body-weight, after the broad-shouldered guards have failed.

Slim, of medium height, full of energy, he was the owner of magnificent thick moustaches and an unquenchable enthusiasm. One day someone brought a new double-barreled gun, and with a single shot he downed a kite, remarking, "Excellent gun…" Colonel Singh was then about ninety-two years old, and he still loved to show off. In short, his style of life entirely corresponded to his surname, which meant "lion."

In the seventeenth century, one of his ancestors saved the maharaja of Bikaner from a tiger and was awarded the honorary title "Singh." Traditionally this was intended only for Kshatriyas, but their ancestor was apparently a special Brahmin, and the title passed to descendants, becoming a surname.

The tales of Gulab Singh breathed of the glory of epic battles, of the sharp aroma of adventures and mortal dangers; in them there was the bitterness of defeats and the sweetness of the nectar of victories. The grandfather lived a long life resembling a tropical storm or cyclone: gusty winds and downpours alternated with sunny days and total calm, and Gulab Singh enjoyed every minute.

…The grandson's full name reads: "Shailendra Bahadur Singh Sharma." Omitting the honorific "Singh" that his grandfather wore with pride, he signs his books simply "Shailendra Sharma." Over time, people began to address him by the name fully consonant with the path he had chosen: "Guruji," Teacher.

The Gangster, the Paan, and a Visit to Prison

In his last year of schooling, the surroundings of Gwalior and several other regions — Bhind and Morena — became famous throughout India thanks to the bandit formations of dacoits. The two most famous bandit leaders, Mohar Singh and Madho Singh, preferred to be called "baagis" — rebels. They did not rob the poor, but in the best traditions of Robin Hood took the wealthy as hostages and distributed the ransom among the needy rural inhabitants.

In the end, in 1972, Mohar Singh and his comrades surrendered to the famous socialist leader Jayaprakash Narayan under an agreement confirmed by Indira Gandhi herself. This agreement freed them from the death penalty. However, all legal obligations and formalities had to be observed in accordance with Indian law, and the bandits were temporarily housed in the Gwalior prison. The news made a sensation; the newspapers even published an interview with Mohar Singh.

"The dispute over my land allotment dragged on so long that in 1958 I rebelled against the system and was forced to take up arms to fight for justice. We were driven into a corner by the exhausting legal system, which deprived me and many other people of basic legal rights." After Mohar Singh killed a man, the price on his head reached 300,000 rupees. For that time, this was a quite large sum.

…Abandoning his homework, he read the interview and imagined a giant — a modern super-villain, a living hero of the comic books. The story captivated him incredibly; Mohar Singh was a living legend, and he had to see this dacoit! One of his uncles was a state prosecutor and often came from Morena to Gwalior prison to prepare the dacoits' case for court. When the uncle visited them on his next trip, he addressed him: "Take me with you to the prison, I want to see these famous bandits, who took hundreds of lives…" The uncle objected hesitantly: "You're too young to look at criminals." But he was unyielding, and in the end it was decided that in a couple of days a police jeep would be sent for him. Other family members were not at all inspired by this idea, and he set off to the prison alone.

On arrival it turned out that the uncle was very busy. Buried in tons of folders and papers together with other representatives of the prosecution, he was working over the bandits' cases. One of the junior officers undertook to conduct the tour and show the nephew the principal "characters."

In the prison yard, his eyes met an actual picnic: some bandits were doing push-ups and exercises; others were getting oil massages; food and sweets were being prepared in gigantic cauldrons right there, under the supervision of numerous servants, and the bored police were more like spectators than guards. The whole thing resembled a big festival simply taking place in a prison. The escorting officer pointed out the most famous bandits and even introduced him to some of them. On the people's faces lay the imprint of an utterly singular, calm self-assurance bordering on a readiness to take life without the slightest hesitation. Their eyes were empty and expressionless; the gaze moved slowly and appraisingly, like that of cattle-traders impassively evaluating the state of a herd. He felt like a lamb. To see, in one place, such a concentration of criminals who had taken so many human lives was extremely uncomfortable. The atmosphere of the prison yard no longer seemed festive; within him stirred the muted echo of fear. Standing at the gate, he awaited the police jeep that was to take him home, and was utterly unprepared for the surprise: Mohar Singh was being driven to the hospital for a medical examination in the same jeep. Because the dacoit had surrendered voluntarily, there was no special fuss over security; the prisoner was accompanied by four policemen and an inspector, and everyone looked rather relaxed. It was decided that Mohar Singh would first be taken to the hospital, and then they would drop the prosecutor's nephew at home…

In the car Mohar Singh sat directly opposite. Danger and self-assurance breathed from him. The bandit had a powerful wrestler's body, and his bandit's pride — enormous moustaches — was elaborately twisted into rings. Learning that the youth sitting opposite was the prosecutor's nephew, the dacoit addressed him cordially, calling him "lallu," "young man." He felt even more ill at ease.

The jeep drove up to the hospital. By the gates stood a kiosk selling paan [footnote 46], and the bandit commanded the police inspector: "Stop and buy paan for lallu." The car braked. Lallu, whom the bandit had decided to treat to paan, froze, not daring to object. All got out of the car: four hefty policemen, the strapping Mohar Singh, and the frightened lallu. They ordered the paan. Within a few minutes the news of the presence of Mohar Singh himself spread like wildfire, and townspeople began to gather around their small group. The dacoit, looking at the gawkers as a wolf looks at sheep, addressed him: "Lallu, tamasa te hoge? — do you want to see a show?" and turned to the paan seller: "Give me a glass of water." The seller obeyed, handing him the glass with trembling hands. Draining it in a few gulps, the dacoit made a threatening movement with the empty glass toward the crowd, bellowing: "Well then, you dorks?!" The crowd recoiled like a wave; several people fell to the ground in panic, and within a minute the street was absolutely empty. The dacoit laughed: "See, lallu, do you understand now how it's done?" The lallu had his breath taken away by the show.

[footnote 46: Paan (or pan) — areca nut, lime, and betel leaves; sometimes other substances are added for flavor and breath freshness. The areca itself may be replaced or mixed with tobacco, and the betel leaves are sometimes excluded. The mixture is not swallowed but spat out.]

The rest of the trip passed without incident. The bandit chief was escorted to the hospital, and he himself was driven home. The adventure was rather alarming and exciting, but on that day he saw something unique: the power of human will cutting through the will of others, like a knife passing through soft butter. A will that creates both a man and his reputation.

…In the archive of the district police department of Bhinda there is still a battered folder of documents. The yellowed pages of the dossier dryly state that Mohar Singh was "a ruthless, cruel, and hardened criminal, charged with 325 crimes, including 85 murders." Having served eight years in prison, the former bandit became the chairman of the BJP-supported [footnote 47] municipality of Nagar in his home town of Mahegaon, but he never came to love politics. In a newspaper interview he said openly: "I understood that there are far greater criminals — politicians." In 2020, the man who could scatter a crowd with an empty glass died at the age of ninety-two.

[footnote 47: BJP — Bharatiya Janata Party, "Indian People's Party" — one of the two principal political parties in India, in power since 2014 under the leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi.]

…Reading of Mohar Singh's death, he remembered the taste of the paan, the frightened crowd, and the triumphant laughter of the brigand. By karmic laws those who have taken human lives must be judged by the astral court, but for some reason it seemed to him that the spirit of Mohar Singh was so strong that he could withstand any verdict, even astral.

Past Lives. Mahadev

In Hinduism, the tradition of shaving a child's first hair is very important, almost obligatory. The mundan ceremony is one of the sixteen rituals of purification known as Shodasha Samskara. It is believed that the rite frees the child of all the negative of past life and contributes to mental and spiritual development, for the hair with which the child was born preserves karmic memories, so it is better to remove them quickly. If they are not cut, the child may fail to "accept" the "new" parents. Recurring memories in small children of strange places, names, or events are usually understood as memories of past life. At times these flashes of memory cause distress and must be "healed."

There is a belief that if one seats a child on a potter's wheel and turns it counterclockwise seven or eleven times, memories of past lives will be erased. And then all the experiences of previous incarnations, together with attachments, pain, desires, and mental imprints, will exert no slowing effect on the soul's development in the present life.

The Mundan ceremony took place when he was three or four months old, not too early and not too late. He showed no signs of connection with the past, but from the age of five he constantly repeated two words (or names): Bartoli and Martoli, and no one knew what they meant. Playing with the dogs, he gave them these names; the words were abracadabra, magical codes, mysterious mantras from the past (or the future). In Gujarat, Odisha, and Uttarakhand there were several small villages with such names, but no one was going to look for any clues or to investigate possible connections.

At about the same age he declared that he would write books when he grew up. What these future works would be about, he was hard-pressed to say. Probably comics, since to imagine that some other genre could interest him he could not. He also informed all who were interested that he would live in a huge house resembling a palace and surrounded by a wall, where nearby there would be three buildings and a pond with steps. And there would be people there, many people. Over the next three or four years, during family celebrations, he told the guests about future palaces… No, he did not claim he would become a king, since he already was the rightful owner of this place. To say exactly where this palace was located, and on what business visitors would come to him, he could not — but his confidence that this would happen was unshakable. Marveling at the scope of the child's imagination, the adults smiled, and of course no one took these stories seriously. But he was unyielding. Sometimes sadhus came to his uncle, a well-known Ayurvedic physician, and from time to time the uncle himself visited these ascetics — and would take him along. The sadhus often smoked chillums [footnote 48], filled not with tobacco or hashish but with some Ayurvedic herbs. One day, when he was eight or nine years old, a sadhu gave him a pipe to clean. The smooth ceramic pipe lay in his palm so naturally, as though he had held it before. A strange sensation came over him, as if something inside whispered: "This was part of your life, do you remember?"

And then there was the effect of fire. From an early age, every time he sat before a fire his mind would become empty, focused only on the dancing flame, and only the involuntary movements of his half-closed eyes echoed that dance. Such a trance could go on for hours. There was nothing unique in this, since every human being descends from gatherers and hunters, long subject to the hypnotic power of the most ancient of gods — Fire. And yet… The script of every destiny is written before the body's birth. Subconsciously and consciously he knew of the path that lay ahead, and of who he had been. But there were no words to express this. Moreover, sharing these feelings and thoughts with anyone would mean becoming vulnerable, over-exposed. By the age of ten he stopped mentioning palaces, visitors, and future books. All this was too personal.

[footnote 48: Chillum, or chilam — a smoking pipe, traditionally made of clay or soft stone. Widely used in India by ascetics and sadhus.]

In the summer of 1971 he turned fourteen, and the whole family went off to vacation in the Himalayas. The wife of his eldest brother was a princess of Jamnagar, and her family owned the Devaldhar estate not far from Bageshwar, to which they had been invited.

The Himalayan range, crowned by two majestic peaks — Trishul and Nanda Devi — was clearly visible for kilometers around, and the panorama took one's breath away. The upper terrace of the great house occupied almost three hundred acres of apple orchards; the idyllic, picturesque view was framed by distant snowy peaks. He usually climbed to the upper tier of the fruit barn and for hours enjoyed the stupendous views. In the mornings the Himalayas turned golden, and at twilight they were plunged into the reddish-gold liquid amber of sunset — a fantastic spectacle. The magical time spent at the estate flew by unnoticed: the family was preparing to return to Gwalior, and he already felt nostalgia for the place.

One day after lunch he went out onto the terrace. And, looking at the already familiar silhouettes of the peaks, he suddenly understood that he was looking at the Lord Shiva Himself. With one foot Mahadev [footnote 49] stood on the peak of Nanda Devi, with the other He trod the Trishul. Closing his eyes, he shook his head. When he opened them, the tangled locks of Shiva's hair spread across the sky like a flame. The hips of Mahadev were girt with a white tiger-skin, and the garland of skulls on His neck had shifted slightly from the movement of His right hand. Gripping a golden trident, Mahadev gazed straight at him; the deity's third eye was closed.

[footnote 49: Mahadev — one of the many names of Shiva, in Sanskrit "great god." Shiva is called Mahadev because all worship him: from the devas (gods) to the asuras (demons).]

The vision produced the strangest effect: he felt that some force was drawing him toward Shiva, that he was about to attain those heights on which the Lord of the Universe stood. And then the apparition vanished. Was this a play of the imagination? Hard to say. He was overcome by a feeling of disquiet, as though he had to act immediately. But nothing remained for him to do but to load with the rest of his family into the car and to return to worldly life.

Three months later the unusual vision repeated itself. That day, returning from school, he ate his lunch mechanically and lay down on the bed in his room, looking through a new comic. Leafing absent-mindedly through the pages, he suddenly saw the same vivid picture — Shiva rising above the Himalayas — and this vision lasted about a minute. Sitting up on the bed, he intended to set off for Bageshwar at once, convinced that Shiva was waiting for him. But it was impossible to explain all this to the adults, and the impulse had to be suppressed.

As subsequent events would show, he did not need to rush to the feet of Mahadev. Twenty-five years later, the Destroyer would come to him himself, and this time the hips of the Lord of the Universe would be wrapped in elephant-skin, the third eye would be half-open, and the sweat of the heated Deity would touch his own skin, leaving forever an invisible mark.

Blessings and Curses

His uncle, the well-known Ayurvedic physician, had many interesting acquaintances and visitors. One of them, Shradhanand Brahmacharya [footnote 50], was a disciple of a most famous guru from Tarapith, the mystical tantric smashan of Bengal. Besides being a realized tantric himself, Shradhanand was a masterful storyteller — and he had a great many tales.

Years later, after many travels, conversations, and meetings with various sadhus, gathering all that he had learned, he came to the conclusion that all religions are nothing but retold legends grown out of magic and spells. But at that time, being a ninth-grader, he listened to the adults' stories with wide-open eyes and a pounding heart.

[footnote 50: Brahmacharya — one who practices brahmacharya, the code of life according to the Vedic scriptures. May also be a surname or title in West Bengal, Assam, and Bangladesh. Brahmacharya is control of body and mind through ascetic exercises and disciplines. In Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist monastic traditions, brahmacharya implies, among other things, the renunciation of sex and marriage.]

One day Shradhanand told of a ritual devoted to Hanuman [footnote 51] that grants great wisdom and unusual abilities. The tale came straight from a trunk of dark fairy-stories. Preparation for this puja is rather complex, and the few who succeeded in performing the ritual were not ordinary people. Only experienced aghoris [footnote 52] and tantrics could perform it correctly, observing the safety measures. If something went wrong, the consequences were irreversible and deadly. This puja was not meant for commoners; only kings (and later major politicians) could set such a task. The gift received could endow a man with the extraordinary capacity to set in motion, or to reverse, a chain of large-scale events.

[footnote 51: Hanuman — one of the Hindu gods and a central figure of the Ramayana, the ardent devotee of Rama and his companion. One of the Seven Immortals and the son of the wind-god Vayu. He is widely known across South Asia, from Thailand and Cambodia to Indonesia. Possessing extraordinary strength and might, he is revered as]

One of the principal components of the ritual is a thick flatbread, called simply "sweet roti [footnote 53]," prepared from specially selected flour. A cloth was spread out, unsifted flour and ingredients poured onto it; the dough was kneaded in mid-air so it would not touch a hard surface (the earth). And then baked over an open fire… Various rites followed, culminating in a summoning of Hanuman. If everything was done correctly, He would appear and grant what was desired.

[footnote 51 cont.: the ideal devotee; he is often worshipped by those who observe celibacy.]
[footnote 52: Aghori (in Sanskrit "aghora" means "fearless") — a monastic order of Indian ascetics worshipping Shiva, most often in His wrathful form of Bhairava. This tantric, non-Puranic form of Shaivism arose between the 7th and 8th centuries CE. Aghoris often live at cremation grounds, smear their bodies with ash, use human bones as ornaments and ritual cups from skulls called kapalas. Participation in the last rites contradicts the postulates of orthodox Hinduism. Nevertheless, aghori gurus enjoy great respect and possess the power of healing, obtained through the performance of rites, strict discipline, and practices of renunciation. As followers of Shiva, the aghoris trace their origin to Baba Kinaram, a sage and ascetic who is believed to have lived 150 years, leaving the body in the second half of the 18th century.]
[footnote 53: Roti — an Indian flatbread, also called "chapati." Prepared from whole-grain flour and water.]

Shradhanand Brahmacharya claimed that he had once successfully performed the ritual, but never specified the purpose, nor what had been obtained as a result.

The main condition of a successful offering is the bhiksha given by representatives of three different castes. In Russian there is no term close to the concept of "bhiksha"; in approximate translation it is something given voluntarily. The bhiksha for this ritual (which had no name and was known as "THE puja of Hanuman") consisted of three portions of flour for the flatbread, taken from a eunuch, a sweeper, and a prostitute.

The first part of the flour could only be given by a hijra — a person whose nature was neither unequivocally male nor female. And it had to be given voluntarily. The blessing of hijras grants prosperity, so they are by tradition invited to various festivals. But such blessings must come from the heart, and therefore happen only very rarely.

The second portion of flour must be given by one who strictly followed the dharma [footnote 54] of his caste. It is held that sweeper-bhangis still live by the laws of dharma. They will never eat on someone else's veranda or on anyone else's territory; by tradition they take food in the street. But if a sweeper accepts an invitation to eat in the home or yard of a person to whom he feels affection, that person may count on prosperity and well-being.

The last part of the flour must be given by a woman who has never thought of a man (in the romantic, erotic sense). Therefore the flour is taken from the house of a prostitute, in whose life there is no "special man."

Years later, the Guru told him: "Always treat prostitutes, eunuchs, and sweepers with respect — all of them possess special powers." Good manners are useful in general, but with members of these three castes, as well as with yogis and sadhus, one is recommended to behave with particular respect. One never knows who these people really are; among them mighty beings may live, as history has confirmed many times.

A prostitute's heartfelt blessing can change a life. Take, for example, the story of Vivekananda. Narendranath Datta, known as Swami Vivekananda, was invited by Maharaja Ajit Singh Bahadur, ruler of the principality of Khetri in Rajasthan. Although versions of this story differ — some claim the incident took place at the court of the ruler of Jaipur — in any case, it is told that in the Pink City a festival of dance was organized, and several prostitutes had been invited there as singers and dancers. Learning of the presence of sex-workers, Vivekananda refused to come to the festival. It was said that he locked himself in his room. In any case, he expressed indignation and revulsion, and one of the priestesses of love noticed it.

[footnote 54: Dharma — "duty," ethical law, the laws of fundamental support of life, virtue, righteousness. One of the four components of the goals of life (according to Hinduism), dharma denotes behavior consonant with the "right path" that makes life and the Universe possible.]

She sang a bhajan, a poem by the great Surdas: "I am as the Lord has made me; it is not my fault that I sell myself for money…" Further it sang that the worldly will not touch one whose virtue is great. Allegorically, in the words of this song, the woman was asking why he — a sannyasi [footnote 55] — was afraid of her. Vivekananda was moved to tears. Falling to his knees, he touched the feet of the woman: "Ma, please forgive me and bless me!" She did so. After this small episode the life of Vivekananda changed greatly, and today he is venerated as a legendary personality.

The Guru added then: "The blessing of a prostitute differs greatly from the blessing of spiritual leaders, who today are more politicians than saints… Prostitutes are nobler and more honest."

life_events.event: At eight or nine years old, a sadhu gives Shailendra a chillum to clean; the ceramic pipe lies in his palm 'as if he had held it before,' and an inner whisper says 'this was part of your life, do you remember?'
From early childhood, sitting before fire would put Shailendra into an hours-long trance — the ancient gathering-and-hunting hypnosis of Fire, the most ancient god.
By the age of ten, Shailendra stops sharing his prophetic stories of palaces, visitors, and future books — they are too personal.

Summer 1971 — the family vacations in the Himalayas at the Devaldhar estate near Bageshwar (owned by his eldest brother's wife's Jamnagar royal family); fourteen-year-old Shailendra spends days on the apple-orchard terrace gazing at Trishul and Nanda Devi.

First vision of Shiva on the Himalayan terrace at Devaldhar: Mahadev stands with one foot on Nanda Devi and one on the Trishul peak; white tiger-skin around his hips, garland of skulls on his neck, golden trident in hand, third eye closed; tangled locks spread like flame across the sky; Mahadev's gaze pulls Shailendra toward him.

Three months later in his Gwalior bedroom, the second Shiva-vision recurs for a minute while he is lying with a comic; he wants to rush to Bageshwar immediately but cannot explain to the adults; vision foretells: 25 years later the Destroyer will come to HIM, this time wrapped in elephant-skin, third eye half-open, and the sweat of the Deity will touch his skin, leaving an invisible mark forever.

As a ninth-grader, Shailendra listens to the tales of Shradhanand Brahmacharya — disciple of a famous Tarapith tantric guru — about the dangerous, unnamed 'THE puja of Hanuman' requiring bhiksha-flour from a hijra, a bhangi (sweeper), and a prostitute.

entity_encounters.encounter: Fourteen-year-old Sharma sees Mahadev standing on the Himalayan peaks of Nanda Devi and Trishul — white tiger-skin, skull-garland, golden trident, third eye closed; the vision pulls him toward Shiva, then disappears.
Three months later the vision recurs in his Gwalior bedroom for about a minute; this time it carries the foretelling that 25 years later Shiva will come to HIM in person.
The realized Tarapith-trained tantric tells the schoolboy Sharma of the great Hanuman puja requiring threefold bhiksha-flour; claims to have once performed the rite himself.

reincarnation_arcs.soul_name: anonymous (Shailendra Sharma's earlier Aghori-tantric incarnation, implied)
reincarnation_arcs.prior_life: An earlier life in which Sharma had handled the chillum-pipe and known Fire intimately — implied by the spontaneous body-memory at age 8-9 ('this was part of your life, do you remember?') and the hour-long fire-trance reflex from early childhood.
reincarnation_arcs.new_life: Born 1957 in Bhopal as Shailendra Sharma; the body-memories of pipe and fire surface unbidden in early childhood.
evidence: Chillum lying 'naturally' in palm at age 8-9 with inner whisper of past handling
Hour-long spontaneous trance before fire from early age, with half-closed eyes mirroring the flame's movements
Sharma's later self-recognition: 'subconsciously and consciously he knew of the path that lay ahead, and of who he had been.'
reincarnation_arcs.guruji_role: Sharma himself is the soul whose past-life sadhana with fire and the chillum surfaces in this body.

entities.name: Lord Shiva
entities.description: Great god worshipped by all from devas to asuras; appears to young Sharma standing one foot on Nanda Devi, one on Trishul, with tiger-skin, skull-garland, golden trident, third eye closed.

entities.name: Trishul (peak)
entities.description: Himalayan peak; Shiva's trident-mountain on which He stands in the vision.

entities.name: Nanda Devi
entities.description: Himalayan peak; Shiva's other foot stands on it in the vision.

entities.name: Devaldhar estate
entities.description: Apple-orchard estate near Bageshwar owned by the Jamnagar royal family (Sharma's sister-in-law's family); site of the first Shiva vision.

entities.name: Bageshwar
entities.description: Town in the Himalayan foothills near Devaldhar.

entities.name: Hanuman
entities.description: Central figure of the Ramayana, ardent devotee of Rama; one of the Seven Immortals; son of the wind-god Vayu; revered from Thailand to Indonesia; ideal devotee, especially worshipped by celibates.

entities.name: Vayu
entities.description: Father of Hanuman.

entities.name: Rama
entities.description: Hero of the Ramayana whose ardent devotee is Hanuman.

entities.name: Shradhanand Brahmacharya
entities.description: Realized tantric, disciple of a famous Tarapith guru; visitor to Sharma's uncle's house; masterful storyteller who once performed THE Hanuman puja.

entities.name: Tarapith
entities.description: Mystical tantric smashan in Bengal.

entities.name: Aghori order
entities.description: Tantric Shaiva order venerating Shiva's wrathful form Bhairava; cremation-ground ascetics; traced to founder Baba Kinaram (mid-18th century).

entities.name: Baba Kinaram
entities.description: Founder of the Aghori line; said to have lived 150 years; left the body in the second half of the 18th century.

entities.name: Bhairava
entities.description: Wrathful form of Shiva worshipped by Aghoris.

entities.name: Surdas
entities.description: Great bhakti poet whose bhajan ('I am as the Lord has made me') the prostitute sang to Vivekananda.

entities.name: Vivekananda
entities.description: Disciple of Ramakrishna; transformed after a prostitute's bhajan-rebuke at Khetri (or Jaipur).

entities.name: Maharaja Ajit Singh Bahadur
entities.description: Ruler of Khetri, Rajasthan; host of Vivekananda.

entities.name: Hijra
entities.description: Person neither unequivocally male nor female; their blessing grants prosperity; one of the three bhiksha-givers for the Hanuman puja.

entities.name: Bhangi (sweeper)
entities.description: Caste of sweepers; said to still live by caste-dharma — never eating on another's veranda; if they consent to eat in your home, prosperity follows; one of the three bhiksha-givers for the Hanuman puja.

entities.name: Prostitute (bhiksha-giver)
entities.description: A woman in whose life there is no 'special man' — supplier of the third portion of flour for the Hanuman puja.

teachings.teaching: All religions are nothing but retold legends grown out of magic and spells.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's mature conclusion after meeting many sadhus — a deflationary view that does NOT deflate the magic itself, only the institutional layers.

teachings.teaching: The script of every destiny is written before the body's birth — subconsciously and consciously the soul knows the path that lies ahead and who it was.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's recognition (via chillum and fire-trance) that his past lives were active in him from earliest childhood.

teachings.teaching: Always treat prostitutes, eunuchs, and sweepers with respect — they all possess special powers; with them, and with yogis and sadhus, behave with particular respect, for among them mighty beings may live.
teachings.lens_note: Guru's instruction to Sharma. The 'special powers' of the three castes correspond to the three bhikshas of THE Hanuman puja — Sharma's later teaching encodes the ritual knowledge as ordinary etiquette.

teachings.teaching: The blessing of a prostitute differs greatly from the blessing of spiritual leaders, who today are more politicians than saints — prostitutes are nobler and more honest.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Guru's blunt verdict; corroborated by the Vivekananda/Surdas-bhajan story.

teachings.teaching: Twenty-five years after the first Himalayan vision, the Destroyer would come to Sharma himself — this time with elephant-skin hips, third eye half-open, and His sweat would touch Sharma's skin, leaving an invisible mark forever.
teachings.lens_note: Foreshadowing: the second Shiva-encounter (ca. 1996) is in-body, intimate, and irreversibly marks Sharma — not a vision but a meeting.
